etymology.story: 1630s, from verbal used of adjective allocate (mid-15c.

meanings.definitionCn: 供应,供给,准备,预防,规定

etymology.story: early 15c., from Latin providere "look ahead, prepare, supply, act with foresight," from pro- "ahead" (see pro-) + videre "to see" (see vision).
1829, from stand (v.) + point (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 最初的,初级的;首要的,主要的,基本的

morphology.mnemonic: 第一/首要 + …的场所/与…有关

etymology.story: 1861, American English, short for primary election (1792, with reference to France; in a U.S.

morphology.mnemonic: 独自/太阳 + 与…有关的/像…的

etymology.story: mid-15c., "pertaining to the sun," from Latin solaris "of the sun," from sol "sun" (see sol).

meanings.definitionCn: 消耗；吃完，喝光；(with)使着迷；烧毁

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French consumer "to consume" (12c.) and directly from Latin consumere "to use up, eat, waste," from com-, intensive prefix (see com-), + sumere "to take," from sub- "under" + emere "to buy, take" (see exempt (adj.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 形成;构成;组织;构造;编制;塑造

morphology.mnemonic: 形状/形态 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., "vital force in plants and animals;" early 15c., "act of creating or making," from Old French formacion "formation, fashioning, creation" (12c.) or directly from Latin formationem (nominative formatio) "a forming, shaping," noun of action or condition from past participle stem of formare "to form," from forma "form, shape" (see form (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 层，层次；膜；[植]压条；放置者，计划者
把…分层堆放；借助压条法；生根繁殖；将（头发）剪成不同层次
三角（形）；三角关系；三角形之物；三人一组

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French triangle (13c.), from Latin triangulum "triangle," noun use of neuter of adjective triangulus "three-cornered, having three angles," from tri- "three" (see tri-) + angulus "corner, angle" (see angle (n.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 偶然的；意外的；无意中的

morphology.mnemonic: unplanned child + 与…有关的/形容词

meanings.definitionCn: 保证；担保；使确信；弄清楚

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French asseurer (12c., Modern French assurer) "to reassure, calm, protect, to render sure," from Vulgar Latin *assecurar, from Latin ad- "to" (see ad-) + securus "safe, secure" (see secure (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 请教，向...咨询，找...商量；查阅，查看

etymology.story: 1520s, from Middle French consulter (16c.), from Latin consultare "consult, take the advice of," frequentative of consulere "to take counsel, meet and consider" (see consultation).

early 14c., "subject or topic on which a person writes or speaks," from Old French tesme (13c., with silent -s- "indicating vowel length" [OED], Modern French thème) and directly from Latin thema "a subject, thesis," from Greek thema "a proposition, subject, deposit," literally "something set down," from root of tithenai "put down, place," from PIE *dhe-mn, from root *dhe- "to put, to do" (see factitious).

meanings.definitionCn: 了不起的；非常轰动的；出色的

morphology.mnemonic: gaudily alluring + 以…方式/副词化

etymology.story: mid-15c., "entirely, purely," from mere (adj.) + -ly (2).

late 14c., from Old French contradiction or directly from Latin contradictionem (nominative contradictio) "a reply, objection, counterargument," noun of action from past participle stem of contradicere, in classical Latin contra dicere "to speak against," from contra "against" (see contra) + dicere "to say, speak" (see diction)..

meanings.definitionCn: 小题大作；忙乱；焦燥；焦急；无事自扰
大惊小怪，大惊小怪的人；小题大作；忙乱
财富， 财产； 丰富

etymology.story: mid-13c., "happiness," also "prosperity in abundance of possessions or riches," from Middle English wele "well-being" (see weal (n.1)) on analogy of health..

meanings.definitionCn: 编者，编辑；社论撰写人；编辑装置

etymology.story: 1640s, "publisher," from Latin editor "one who puts forth," agent noun from editus, past participle of edere "to bring forth, produce" (see edition).
"course of action," late 14c., from mean (n.); sense of "wealth" is first recorded c.

meanings.definitionCn: 有效的,效率高的;有能力的,能胜任的

etymology.story: late 14c., "making, producing immediate effect, active, effective," from Old French efficient and directly from Latin efficientem (nominative efficiens) "effective, efficient, producing, active," present participle of efficere "work out, accomplish" (see effect).

morphology.mnemonic: 开始/初始 + 与…有关的/形容词
不/非/否定 + fortune + 使…/具有…的 + 以…方式/副词化

etymology.story: 1540s, "in an unfortunate manner, by ill-fortune," from unfortunate + -ly (2).

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 站立/设置 + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: 1570s, "standing firm, standing still," from Latin consistentem (nominative consistens), present participle of consistere (see consist).

Old English sweord, swyrd (West Saxon), sword (Northumbrian) "sword," from Proto-Germanic *swerdam (source also of Old Saxon, Old Frisian swerd, Old Norse sverð, Swedish svärd, Middle Dutch swaert, Dutch zwaard, Old High German swert, German Schwert "a sword"), related to Old High German sweran "to hurt," from *swertha-, literally "the cutting weapon," from PIE root *swer- (3) "to cut, pierce."

Contrast with plowshare is from the Old Testament (Isaiah ii:4, Micah iv:3).

early 15c., from Middle French permanent (14c.) or directly from Latin permanentem (nominative permanens) "remaining," present participle of permanere "endure, hold out, continue, stay to the end," from per- "through"  (see per) + manere "stay" (see mansion).

meanings.definitionCn: 赤裸裸地,无遮蔽地;仅仅,勉强,几乎没有

etymology.story: Old English bærlice "openly, clear, public;" see bare (adj.) + -ly (2).
1837, American English, from summary + -ize.

"to crack," mid-15c., chappen (intransitive) "to split, burst open;" "cause to crack" (transitive); perhaps a variant of choppen (see chop (v.), and compare strap/strop), or related to Middle Dutch kappen "to chop, cut," Danish kappe, Swedish kappa "to cut."

early 15c., "gentleness, lightness," from Middle French facilité "easiness, ease," from Latin facilitatem (nominative facilitas) "easiness, ease, fluency, willingness," from facilis "easy" (see facile).

meanings.definitionCn: 横梁；光线；电波；船宽；[计量] 秤杆
发送；以梁支撑；用…照射；流露

morphology.mnemonic: 公民 + ize + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1704, "law which makes a criminal process civil," from civilize + -ation.

morphology.mnemonic: 塑造，虚构 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: early 15c., ficcioun, "that which is invented or imagined in the mind," from Old French ficcion "dissimulation, ruse; invention, fabrication" (13c.) and directly from Latin fictionem (nominative fictio) "a fashioning or feigning," noun of action from past participle stem of fingere "to shape, form, devise, feign," originally "to knead, form out of clay," from PIE *dheigh- "to build, form, knead" (source also of Old English dag "dough;" see dough).

meanings.definitionCn: 持有,拥有;所有权;所有物;(pl.)财产,财富

morphology.mnemonic: to hold as property + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: mid-14c., "act or fact of possessing, a taking possession, occupation," also "thing possessed, that which is possessed," from Old French possession "fact of having and holding; what is possessed;" also "demonic possession," and directly from Latin possessionem (nominative possessio), noun of action from past participle stem of possidere "to possess" (see possess).

meanings.definitionCn: 取代，代替；替换，更换；归还，偿还；把…放回原处

etymology.story: 1590s, "to restore to a previous place or position," from re- "back, again" + place (v.).

morphology.mnemonic: put (someone) in charge + 结果/过程/状态

etymology.story: early 15c., "an agreement," also "a fixing of a date for official business," from Middle French apointement, from apointer (see appoint).

meanings.definitionCn: 熟悉内情的；非常时尚的

etymology.story: exclamation used to introduce a united cheer (as in hip-hip-hurrah), 1827, earlier hep; compare German hepp, to animals a cry to attack game, to mobs a cry to attack Jews (see hep (2)); perhaps a natural sound (such as Latin eho, heus)..

Old English myrge "pleasing, agreeable, pleasant, sweet; pleasantly, melodiously," from Proto-Germanic *murgijaz, which probably originally meant "short-lasting," (compare Old High German murg "short," Gothic gamaurgjan "to shorten"), from PIE *mreghu- "short" (see brief (adj.)).
early 14c., comandur, from Old French comandeor, from comander (see command (v.)).
mid-13c., blomen, from the noun (see bloom (n.1)).

1570s, "intensity of expression," from Latin emphasis, from Greek emphasis "an appearing in, outward appearance;" in rhetoric, "significance, indirect meaning," from emphainein "to present, exhibit, display, let (a thing) be seen; be reflected (in a mirror), become visible," from assimilated form of en "in" (see en- (2)) + phainein "to show" (see phantasm).

meanings.definitionCn: 可行的；可能的；可实行的

etymology.story: "capable of being done, accomplished or carried out," mid-15c., from Anglo-French faisible, from Old French faisable "possible, that may be done; easy, convenient," from fais-, stem of faire "do, make," from Latin facere "to make, do, perform" (see factitious).
"to write down as a direction," mid-15c., from Latin praescribere "write beforehand" (see prescription).

meanings.definitionCn: 潜能；可能性；[电] 电势

morphology.mnemonic: 力量/能力 + …的人/处于…状态 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: late 14c., "possible" (as opposed to actual), from Old French potenciel and directly from Late Latin potentialis "potential," from Latin potentia "power, might, force;" figuratively "political power, authority, influence," from potens "powerful" (see potent).

Old English bean "bean, pea, legume," from Proto-Germanic *bauno (source also of Old Norse baun, Middle Dutch bone, Dutch boon, Old High German bona, German Bohne), and related to Latin faba "bean;" Greek phakos "lentil;" Albanian bathë "horse-bean;" Old Prussian babo, Russian bob "bean," but the original form is obscure; perhaps from a PIE reduplicated base *bha-bha- 

As a metaphor for "something of small value" it is attested from c.

meanings.definitionCn: 惯常的；习惯的，适应的(一般作表语)

etymology.story: late 15c., "made customary, habitual," past participle adjective from accustom (v.)..

meanings.definitionCn: 结合；组合；联合；[化学] 化合

etymology.story: late 14c., combinacyoun, from Old French combination (14c., Modern French combinaison), from Late Latin combinationem (nominative combinatio) "a joining two by two," noun of action from past participle stem of combinare (see combine (v.))..

early 15c., from Middle French obtenir "acquire, obtain" (14c.), from Latin obtinere "hold, hold fast, take hold of, get possession of, acquire," from ob "to" (though perhaps intensive in this case; see ob-) + tenere "to hold" (see tenet).

late 14c., "careful attention to detail," also "desire to know or learn" (originally usually in a bad sense), from Old French curiosete "curiosity, avidity, choosiness" (Modern French curiosité), from Latin curiositatem (nominative curiositas) "desire of knowledge, inquisitiveness," from curiosus (see curious).

meanings.definitionCn: 总共的；粗野的；恶劣的；显而易见的
车间；研讨会；工场；讲习班

etymology.story: 1580s, from work (n.) + shop (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 抵抗,反抗;抗,忍得住,抵制

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + 站立/设置

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French resister "hold out against" (14c.), from Latin resistere "to make a stand against, oppose; to stand back; withstand," from re- "against" (see re-) + sistere "take a stand, stand firm" (see assist).

meanings.definitionCn: [法] 非法的；违法的；违反规则的

etymology.story: 1630s, from French illégal (14c.) or directly from Medieval Latin illegalis, from assimilated form of in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + Latin legalis (see legal).
1610s, "rouse to action," from Latin stimulatus, past participle of stimulare (see stimulation).

late 14c., "pertaining to form or arrangement;" also, in philosophy and theology, "pertaining to the form or essence of a thing," from Old French formal, formel "formal, constituent" (13c.) and directly from Latin formalis, from forma "a form, figure, shape" (see form (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 彩虹；五彩缤纷的排列；幻想
使呈彩虹状；如彩虹般装饰

etymology.story: Old English renboga; see rain (n.) + bow (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 吸收，吸入；沉浸在（工作或学习中）；使……上下湿透

etymology.story: Old English socian (intransitive) "to soak, to lie in liquid," from Proto-Germanic *sukon (source also of West Flemish soken), possibly from PIE *sug-, from root *seue- (2) "to take liquid" (see sup (v.2)).
late 14c., from Old French essorer "fly up, soar," from Vulgar Latin *exaurare "rise into the air," from Latin ex- "out" (see ex-) + aura "breeze, air" (see aura).
1520s, from genitive of sale (compare craftsman, tradesman) + man (n.)..

morphology.mnemonic: 之间/相互 + one who goes with another. + ance

etymology.story: 1783, "intermeddling," from interfere on model of difference, etc.

meanings.definitionCn: 维持；支撑，承担；忍受；供养；证实
招待,款待;使娱乐;使欢乐;容纳,接受

morphology.mnemonic: 之间/相互 + 持有/保持

etymology.story: late 15c., "to keep up, maintain, to keep (someone) in a certain frame of mind," from Middle French entretenir, from Old French entretenir "hold together, stick together, support" (12c.), from entre- "among" (from Latin inter; see inter-) + tenir "to hold" (from Latin tenere; see tenet).
late 14c., from Latin altitudinem (nominative altitudo) "height, altitude," from altus "high" (see old)..
1782, from French classifier, from classe (see class (n.)) + -fier (see -fy).

meanings.definitionCn: 批评的,评论的;危急,紧要的;临界的

root.meaning: one who judges merits of books, plays, etc.

morphology.mnemonic: one who judges merits of books, plays, etc. + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1580s, "censorious," from critic + -al (1).

meanings.definitionCn: 殖民地；移民队；种群；动物栖息地

morphology.mnemonic: 殖民/定居 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: late 14c., "ancient Roman settlement outside Italy," from Latin colonia "settled land, farm, landed estate," from colonus "husbandman, tenant farmer, settler in new land," from colere "to inhabit, cultivate, frequent, practice, tend, guard, respect," from PIE root *kwel- (1) "move around" (source of Latin -cola "inhabitant;" see cycle (n.)).
1580s; see mathematic + -ics.

"four-wheeled vehicle to carry heavy loads," late 15c., from Middle Dutch wagen, waghen, from Proto-Germanic *wagnaz (source also of Old English wægn, Modern English wain, Old Saxon and Old High German wagan, Old Norse vagn, Old Frisian wein, German Wagen), from PIE *wogh-no-, suffixed form of root *wegh- "to carry, to move" (source also of Sanskrit vahanam "vessel, ship," Greek okhos, Latin vehiculum, Old Church Slavonic vozŭ "carriage, chariot," Russian povozka, Lithuanian va žis "a small sledge," Old Irish fen, Welsh gwain "carriage, cart;" see weigh).
"in another place, in other places," c.

meanings.definitionCn: 穿过， 渗入；看穿， 洞察

etymology.story: 1520s, from Latin penetratus, past participle of penetrare "to put or get into, enter into," related to penitus "within, inmost," penus "innermost part of a temple, store of food," penates "household gods."

meanings.definitionCn: 最重要的,主要的n.负责人,校长;资本,本金

etymology.story: 1610s, "pattern, model," from Latin specimen "indication, mark, example, sign, evidence; that by which a thing is known, means of knowing," from specere "to look at" (see scope (n.1)).

meanings.definitionCn: 救济；减轻，解除；安慰；浮雕
方面;[数] 维；尺寸；次元；容积 vt标出尺寸

etymology.story: late 14c., "measurement, size," from Latin dimensionem (nominative dimensio) "a measuring," noun of action from past participle stem of dimetri "to measure out," from dis- (see dis-) + metiri "to measure" (see measure (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: (to)介绍;传入,引进;导言,导论,绪论

morphology.mnemonic: intra + 引导 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., "act of bringing into existence," from Old French introduccion (14c.) and directly from Latin introductionem (nominative introductio) "a leading in," noun of action from past participle stem of introducere "to lead in, bring in; introduce; found, establish; bring forward (as an assertion)," from intro- "inward, to the inside" (see intro-) + ducere "to lead" (see duke (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 向下/移除/取消 + 保持/服务

etymology.story: early 13c., from Old French deservir (Modern French desservir) "deserve, be worthy of, earn, merit," from Latin deservire "serve well," from de- "completely" (see de-) + servire "to serve" (see serve).

meanings.definitionCn: 过程,进程;工序,制作法;工艺

etymology.story: early 14c., "fact of being carried on" (as in in process), from Old French proces "a journey; continuation, development; legal trial" (13c.) and directly from Latin processus "a going forward, advance, progress," from past participle stem of procedere "go forward" (see proceed).

from Old English philosophe, from Latin philosophus "philosopher," from Greek philosophos "philosopher, sage, one who speculates on the nature of things and truth," literally "lover of wisdom," from philos "loving" (see -phile) + sophos "wise, a sage" (see sophist).

1846, "medical treatment of disease," from Modern Latin therapia, from Greek therapeia "curing, healing, service done to the sick; a waiting on, service," from therapeuein "to cure, treat medically," literally "attend, do service, take care of" (see therapeutic)..

meanings.definitionCn: 供选择的；选择性的；交替的
二中择一；供替代的选择

morphology.mnemonic: altern + 使…/具有…的 + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: 1620s, in rhetoric, from Medieval Latin alternativus (see alternative (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 浓度；集中；浓缩；专心；集合

root.meaning: to rest as at a center

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + to rest as at a center + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1630s, "action of bringing to a center," noun of action from verb concentrate (v.).

late 15c., "sodium carbonate," an alkaline substance extracted from certain ashes (now made artificially), from Italian sida (or Medieval Latin soda) "a kind of saltwort," from which soda was obtained, of uncertain origin.
1530s, from Middle French collègue (16c.), from Latin collega "partner in office," from com- "with" (see com-) + leg-, stem of legare "to choose" (see legate).

morphology.mnemonic: 感激/愉悦 + 状态/品质

etymology.story: mid-15c., "good will," from Middle French gratitude (15c.) or directly from Medieval Latin gratitudinem (nominative gratitudo) "thankfulness," from Latin gratus "thankful, pleasing" (see grace (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: (of)赞成，赞许，同意；批准，审议，通过

morphology.mnemonic: 之间/相互 + 做/驱动 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1812, from inter- + action..

morphology.mnemonic: 之间/相互 + one who goes with another.

etymology.story: formerly also enterfere, mid-15c., "to strike against," from Middle French enterferir "exchange blows, strike each other," from entre- "between" (see entre-) + ferir "to strike," from Latin ferire "to knock, strike," related to Latin forare "to bore, pierce" (see bore (v.), and compare punch (v.), which has both the senses "to hit" and "to make a hole in").

morphology.mnemonic: 统治 + 结果/过程/状态

etymology.story: 1640s, "private rooms for the use of one person within a house," from French appartement (16c.), from Italian appartimento, literally "a separated place," from appartere "to separate," from a "to" (see ad-) + parte "side, place," from Latin partem (see part (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 不舒服的；心神不安的；不稳定的

root.meaning: to give ease, mitigate, alleviate, relieve from pain or care

morphology.mnemonic: 不/非/否定 + to give ease, mitigate, alleviate, relieve from pain or care + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: late 13c., "not comforting, causing trouble," from un- (1) "not" + easy (adj.).

morphology.mnemonic: 忍受/容忍 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1530s, of authorities, "to allow without interference," from Latin toleratus, past participle of tolerare (see toleration).

morphology.mnemonic: de + 结果/过程/状态 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1830, from development + -al (1).

morphology.mnemonic: 不足/在…之下 + one's occupation, branch of business

etymology.story: 1721, "to mark underneath or below with a line," from under + line (v.).

morphology.mnemonic: public house with lodging + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: mid-14c., "doing no evil; free from sin, guilt, or moral wrong," from Old French inocent "harmless; not guilty; pure" (12c.), from Latin innocentem (nominative innocens) "not guilty, blameless; harmless; disinterested," from in- "not" (see in- (1)) + nocentem (nominative nocens), present participle of nocere "to harm," from *nok-s-, suffixed form of PIE root *nek- (1) "death" (see necro-).

mid-14c., from Old French compassion "sympathy, pity" (12c.), from Late Latin compassionem (nominative compassio) "sympathy," noun of state from past participle stem of compati "to feel pity," from com- "together" (see com-) + pati "to suffer" (see passion).

meanings.definitionCn: 字母表，字母系统；入门，初步

etymology.story: 1570s, from Late Latin alphabetum (Tertullian), from Greek alphabetos, from alpha + beta.

morphology.mnemonic: 出/从 + 强壮/价值 + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1755, "action of appraising or valuing," from French évaluation, noun of action from évaluer "to find the value of," from é- "out" (see ex-) + valuer (see value (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 结合；装订；有约束力；过紧
捆绑；困境；讨厌的事情；植物的藤蔓
为...表示感激，感谢；欣赏，赏识，评价

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + 价格/价值 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1650s, "to esteem or value highly," from Late Latin appretiatus, past participle of appretiare "to set a price to" (see appraise).

morphology.mnemonic: tooth牙齿 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1590s, from Middle French dental "of teeth" or Medieval Latin dentalis, from Latin dens (genitive dentis) "tooth," from PIE root *dent- (see tooth)..

meanings.definitionCn: 可预言的， 可预报的； 按老一套办事的， 墨守成规的

morphology.mnemonic: 在…之前/预先 + 说/讲 + able

etymology.story: 1820, from predict + -able.
1812 in modern sense, from rail (n.1) + way (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 使成立方形；使自乘二次；量…的体积
团结；重整；恢复；（网球等）连续对打
首要的;最好的,第一流的
发生； 碰巧， 恰好

etymology.story: late 14c., happenen, "to come to pass, occur, come about, be the case," literally "occur by hap, have the (good or bad) fortune (to do, be, etc.);" extension (with verb-formative -n) of the more common hap (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 节日；庆祝，纪念活动；欢乐

morphology.mnemonic: fest + 有…倾向的 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1580s, "a festal day, appointed day of festive celebration," short for festival day (late 14c.), from Old French festival (adj.) "suitable for a feast; solemn, magnificent, joyful, happy," and directly from Medieval Latin festivalis "of a church holiday," from festum "festival, holiday" (see festivity).

meanings.definitionCn: 温和的；轻微的；淡味的；文雅的；不含有害物质的的

etymology.story: Old English milde "gentle, merciful," from Proto-Germanic *milthjaz- (source also of Old Norse mildr, Old Saxon mildi, Old Frisian milde, Middle Dutch milde, Dutch mild, Old High German milti, German milde "mild," Gothic mildiþa "kindness"), from PIE *meldh-, from root *mel- "soft," with derivatives referring to soft or softened materials (source also of Greek malthon "weakling," myle "mill;" Latin molere "to grind;" Old Irish meldach "tender;" Sanskrit mrdh "to neglect," also "to be moist").

meanings.definitionCn: 侧面；轮廓；外形；剖面；简况

etymology.story: 1530s, in reference to subjects of sovereigns or governments, from Middle French loyal, from Old French loial, leal "of good quality; faithful; honorable; law-abiding; legitimate, born in wedlock," from Latin legalem, from lex "law." In most cases it has displaced Middle English leal, which is from the same French source.

morphology.mnemonic: to esteem (something) more than others + ance

etymology.story: mid-15c., "advancement in position or status;" 1650s as "act of prefering," from Middle French preference (14c., Modern French préférence), from Medieval Latin preferentia, from past participle stem of Latin praeferrere (see prefer).

meanings.definitionCn: 冲击， 碰撞；效果； 影响

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French opportunite (13c.) and directly from Latin opportunitatem (nominative opportunitas) "fitness, convenience, suitableness, favorable time," from opportunus (see opportune).

meanings.definitionCn: 活跃的，敏捷的，积极的；在活动中的

etymology.story: mid-14c., "given to worldly activity" (opposed to contemplative or monastic), from Old French actif (12c.) or directly from Latin activus, from actus (see act (n.)).
late 14c., from which + ever.

meanings.definitionCn: 招致（失败、危险等）；向…献殷勤；设法获得

etymology.story: "woo, offer homage," as one does at court, 1570s; see court (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 磨粉机； 磨坊；作坊； 工厂

etymology.story: mid-13c., from Anglo-French buquet "bucket, pail," from Old French buquet "bucket," which is from Frankish or some other Germanic source, or a diminutive of cognate Old English buc "pitcher, bulging vessel," originally "belly" (buckets were formerly of leather as well as wood), both from West Germanic *buh- (source also of Dutch buik, Old High German buh, German Bauch "belly"), possibly from a variant of PIE root *beu-, *bheu- "to grow, swell" (see bull (n.2)).

meanings.definitionCn: 灵活的；柔韧的；易弯曲的

etymology.story: early 15c., "capable of being bent; mentally or spiritually pliant," from Middle French flexible or directly from Latin flexibilis "that may be bent, pliant, flexible, yielding;" figuratively "tractable, inconstant," from flex-, past participle stem of flectere "to bend," which is of uncertain origin.
1660s, via French attitude (17c.), from Italian attitudine "disposition, posture," also "aptness, promptitude," from Late Latin aptitudinem (nominative aptitudo; see aptitude).

meanings.definitionCn: 相对的；有关系的；成比例的
亲戚；相关物；[语] 关系词；亲缘植物

root.meaning: stand in some relation; have reference or respect

morphology.mnemonic: stand in some relation; have reference or respect + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: early 15c., "having reference," from Middle French relatif and directly from Late Latin relativus (see relative (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: [生物] 器官；机构；风琴；管风琴；嗓音

etymology.story: fusion of late Old English organe, and Old French orgene (12c.), both meaning "musical instrument," both from Latin organa, plural of organum "a musical instrument," from Greek organon "implement, tool for making or doing; musical instrument; organ of sense, organ of the body," literally "that with which one works," from PIE *werg-ano-, from root *werg- "to do" (source also of Greek ergon "work," orgia "religious performances;" Armenian gorc "work;" Avestan vareza "work, activity;" Gothic waurkjan, Old English wyrcan "to work," Old English weorc "deed, action, something done;" Old Norse yrka "work, take effect").
1560s, "conduct or behavior toward someone or something," from treat (v.) + -ment.

morphology.mnemonic: a plural number + 主义/学说/运动

etymology.story: 1818, as a term in church administration, from plural + -ism.

morphology.mnemonic: in + 流动 + ance + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: "powerful, having influence," 1650s, from Medieval Latin influentialis, from influentia (see influence (n.)).

early 14c., from Anglo-Latin biblia, Old French bible (13c.) "the Bible," also any large book generally, from Medieval and Late Latin biblia "the Bible" (neuter plural interpreted as feminine singular), from phrase biblia sacra "holy books," a translation of Greek ta biblia to hagia "the holy books." The Latin word is from Greek biblion "paper, scroll," the ordinary word for "book," originally a diminutive of byblos "Egyptian papyrus."

The Greek word perhaps is from Byblos, the name of the Phoenician port from which Egyptian papyrus was exported to Greece (modern Jebeil, in Lebanon; compare parchment).

meanings.definitionCn: 酒，含酒精饮料；溶液；液体；烈酒
可敬的；荣誉的， 光荣的

root.meaning: sum of qualities that define a person

morphology.mnemonic: sum of qualities that define a person + ic

etymology.story: adjective and noun both first attested 1660s, from character + -istic on model of Greek kharakteristikos.

meanings.definitionCn: 透明的，透光的；易理解的；明显的

morphology.mnemonic: 穿越/转变 + 平等 + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: early 15c., from Medieval Latin transparentem (nominative transparens), present participle of transparere "show light through," from Latin trans- "through" (see trans-) + parere "come in sight, appear" (see appear).
"part of a country distant from the coast," 1796, from interior (adj.); meaning "internal part, inside" is from 1828.

mid-15c., intransitive probably from Anglo-French or Old French reuser, ruser, originally used in English of hawks shaking the feathers of the body, but like many hawking terms it is of obscure origin.

morphology.mnemonic: 日/天 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: mid-14c., "book of church services," from Anglo-French jurnal, from Old French jornel, "a day; time; a day's travel or work" (12c., Modern French journal), properly "that which takes place daily," noun use of adjective meaning "daily, of the day," from Late Latin diurnalis "daily" (see diurnal).

morphology.mnemonic: in + volve + 结果/过程/状态

etymology.story: 1706, from involve + -ment..

meanings.definitionCn: 集合；会议；装配；(美)洲议会的众议院

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + 相似/聚集 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: early 13c., "perception of sound by ear, action of listening," verbal noun from hear (v.).

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + quire + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., "act of obtaining," from Old French acquisicion (13c.) or directly from Latin acquisitionem (nominative acquisitio), noun of action from past participle stem of acquirere "get in addition, accumulate," from ad- "extra" (see ad-) + quaerere "to seek to obtain" (see query (v.)).
1610s, from Italian vulcano "burning mountain," from Latin Vulcanus "Vulcan," Roman god of fire, also "fire, flames, volcano" (see Vulcan).

meanings.definitionCn: 严格的；僵硬的，死板的；坚硬的；精确的

etymology.story: early 15c., from Latin rigidus "hard, stiff, rough, severe," from rigere "be stiff," from PIE *reig- "stretch (tight), bind tightly, make fast" (source also of Old Irish riag "torture," Middle High German ric "band, string").
"time limit," 1920, American English newspaper jargon, from dead (adj.) + line (n.).
mid-15c., from so (adv.) + past participle of call (v.).
1550s, "pleasing to the mind," also "full of gratitude, disposed to repay favors bestowed," from obsolete adjective grate "agreeable, pleasant," from Latin gratus "pleasing" (see grace (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: [电子] 芯片；筹码；碎片；(食物的) 小片; 薄片

etymology.story: early 15c., "to chip" (intransitive, of stone); from Old English forcippian "to pare away by cutting, cut off," verbal form of cipp "small piece of wood" (see chip (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 下巴；聊天；引体向上动作
用下巴夹住；与…聊天；在单杠上作引体向上动作
感情的， 情绪的； 引起情感的， 表示情感的； 情绪激动的， 易动感情的

morphology.mnemonic: em + 状态/行为/过程 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1821, "pertaining to emotion," from emotion + -al (1).

morphology.mnemonic: 工作 + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1922, "pertaining to operation," from operation + -al (1).

meanings.definitionCn: 卷入,陷入,连累;包含,含有,涉及

etymology.story: late 14c., "envelop, surround; make cloudy or obscure," from Old French involver and directly from Latin involvere "envelop, surround, overwhelm," literally "roll into," from in- "in" (see in- (2)) + volvere "to roll" (see volvox).

meanings.definitionCn: 显而易见的，显著的；值得注意的

etymology.story: 1796, "worthy of notice," from notice (n.) + -able.

early 14c., transitive and intransitive, from Old French assembler "come together, join, unite; gather" (11c.), from Latin assimulare "to make like, liken, compare; copy, imitate; feign, pretend," later "to gather together," from ad- "to" (see ad-) + simulare "to make like" (see simulation).
Old English blæstan "to blow, belch forth," from the root of blast (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 系列，连续；[电] 串联；级数；丛书

etymology.story: 1610s, "a number or set of things of one kind arranged in a line," from Latin series "row, chain, series, sequence, succession," from serere "to join, link, bind together, arrange, attach, put; join in speech, discuss," from PIE root *ser- (3) "to line up, join" (source also of Sanskrit sarat- "thread," Greek eirein "to fasten together in rows," Gothic sarwa (plural) "armor, arms," Old Norse sörve "necklace of stringed pearls," Old Irish sernaid "he joins together," Welsh ystret "row").
1640s, "tangled," from past participle adjective from complicate.

meanings.definitionCn: 耕作， 栽培； 养殖；培养， 教养； 磨炼

etymology.story: early 17c., from Medieval Latin cultivatus, past participle of cultivare "to cultivate," from Late Latin cultivus "tilled," from Latin cultus (see cult).

meanings.definitionCn: [数] 极大，最大限度；最大量

etymology.story: late 14c., "to bring to mind by description," also "to symbolize, serve as a sign or symbol of; serve as the type or embodiment of;" from Old French representer "present, show, portray" (12c.), from Latin repraesentare "make present, set in view, show, exhibit, display," from re-, intensive prefix (see re-), + praesentare "to present," literally "to place before" (see present (v.)).

1610s, " to enter or be put in rivalry with," from Middle French compéter "be in rivalry with" (14c.), or directly from Late Latin competere "strive in common," in classical Latin "to come together, agree, to be qualified," later, "strive together," from com- "together" (see com-) + petere "to strive, seek, fall upon, rush at, attack" (see petition (n.)).

1570s, "form of speech of a region or group," from Middle French dialecte, from Latin dialectus "local language, way of speaking, conversation," from Greek dialektos "talk, conversation, speech;" also "the language of a country, dialect," from dialegesthai "converse with each other," from dia- "across, between" (see dia-) + legein "speak" (see lecture (n.))..

root.meaning: to reckon for money given or received, render a reckoning,

morphology.mnemonic: to reckon for money given or received, render a reckoning, + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: mid-15c., "accounting officer, one who renders accounts," from Old French acuntant (Modern French accomptant), from present participle of accompter (see account (v.)).

"close-fitting garment covering the foot and lower leg," 1580s, from stock "leg covering, stocking" (late 15c.), from Old English stocu "sleeve," related to Old English stocc "trunk, log" (see stock (n.1)).

late 14c., "ability, opportunity, means, resources," from Old French faculte "skill, accomplishment, learning" (14c., Modern French faculté) and directly from Latin facultatem (nominative facultas) "power, ability, capability, opportunity; sufficient number, abundance, wealth," from *facli-tat-s, from facilis (see facile).

1570s, "marked by deliberation," from Latin consideratus, past participle of considerare (see consider).

mid-14c., "travel from one place to another," from Anglo-French journeyer, Old French journoiier "work by day; go, walk, travel," from journée "a day's work or travel" (see journey (n.)).

late Old English planete, from Old French planete (Modern French planète), from Late Latin planeta, from Greek planetes, from (asteres) planetai "wandering (stars)," from planasthai "to wander," of unknown origin, possibly from PIE *pele- (2) "flat, to spread" on notion of "spread out." So called because they have apparent motion, unlike the "fixed" stars.

爬行；毛骨悚然的感觉；谄媚者
用带捆绑；用皮条抽打；约束
葡萄；葡萄酒；葡萄树；葡萄色

etymology.story: mid-13c., "a grape, a berry of the vine," also collective singular, from Old French grape "bunch of grapes, grape" (12c.), probably a back-formation from graper "steal; grasp; catch with a hook; pick (grapes)," from a Frankish or other Germanic word, from Proto-Germanic *krappon "hook," from a group of Germanic words meaning "bent, crooked, hooked" (cognates: Middle Dutch crappe, Old High German krapfo "hook;" also see cramp (n.2)).
mid-15c., "the proving of guilt," from Late Latin convictionem (nominative convictio) "proof, refutation," noun of action from past participle stem of convincere (see convince).
Old English scolere "student," from Medieval Latin scholaris, noun use of Late Latin scholaris "of a school," from Latin schola (see school (n.1)).

1878, shortening of graphic formula (see graphic).

mid-13c., from Old Norse lan, related to lja "to lend," from Proto-Germanic *laikhwniz (source also of Old Frisian len "thing lent," Middle Dutch lene, Dutch leen "loan, fief," Old High German lehan, German Lehn "fief, feudal tenure"), originally "to let have, to leave (to someone)," from PIE *leikw- "to leave" (see relinquish).

1610s, "not to know, to be ignorant of," from French ignorer "be unaware of" (14c.), or directly from Latin ignorare "not to know, be unacquainted; take no notice of, disregard" (see ignorant).

mid-14c., agent noun from own (v.)..

Old English halig "holy, consecrated, sacred; godly; ecclesiastical," from Proto-Germanic *hailaga- (source also of Old Norse heilagr, Danish hellig, Old Frisian helich "holy," Old Saxon helag, Middle Dutch helich, Old High German heilag, German heilig, Gothic hailags "holy"), from PIE *kailo- "whole, uninjured" (see health).

Old English preost probably shortened from the older Germanic form represented by Old Saxon and Old High German prestar, Old Frisian prestere, all from Vulgar Latin *prester "priest," from Late Latin presbyter "presbyter, elder," from Greek presbyteros (see Presbyterian).
early 15c., from Latin permissionem (nominative permissio), noun of action from past participle stem of permittere (see permit (v.))..

安排，计划；编制目录；将……列入计划表

etymology.story: 1580s, "sending of mail by post;" 1650s as "cost of sending something by mail," from post (n.3) + -age.

重要的；物质的，实质性的；肉体的

etymology.story: late 14c., "substance, matter from which a thing is made," from material (adj.)..

1580s, "the whole world, cosmos, the totality of existing things," from Old French univers (12c.), from Latin universum "all things, everybody, all people, the whole world," noun use of neuter of adjective universus "all together, all in one, whole, entire, relating to all," literally "turned into one," from unus "one" (see one) + versus, past participle of vertere "to turn" (see versus)..

late 14c., "expound the meaning of, render clear or explicit," from Old French interpreter "explain; translate" (13c.) and directly from Latin interpretari "explain, expound, understand," from interpres "agent, translator," from inter- "between" (see inter-) + second element of uncertain origin, perhaps related to Sanskrit prath- "to spread abroad," PIE *per- (5) "to traffic in, sell" (see pornography).

etymology.story: "a forcing of a fluid into a body" (with a syringe, etc.), early 15c., from Old French iniection (14c.) or directly from Latin iniectionem (nominative iniectio) "a throwing in," noun of action from past participle stem of inicere "to throw in or on" (see inject)..
late 14c., from Old French envier "envy, be envious of," from envie (see envy (n.)).

1650s, from French hutte "a cottage" (16c.), from Middle High German hütte "cottage, hut," probably from Proto-Germanic *hudjon-, which is related to the root of Old English hydan "to hide," from PIE *keudh-, from root (s)keu- "to cover, conceal" (see hide (n.1)).

early 15c., from Old French invincible (14c.) or directly from Latin invincibilis "unconquerable," from in- "not" (see in- (1)) + vincibilis "to be gained, easily maintained, conquerable," from vincere "to overcome" (see victor).

1620s, "reckon as an abatement or deduction," from Old French desconter (13c., Modern French décompter), from Medieval Latin discomputare, from dis- (see dis-) + computare "to count" (see count (v.)).

"to bend like a hook," c.

mid-14c., "where;" early 15c.

in the modern sense, 1845, American English, from base (n.) + ball (n.1).

meanings.definitionCn: 间隔,间歇;(幕间或工间)休息

etymology.story: early 14c., "time elapsed between two actions or events," from Old French intervalle "interval, interim" (14c.), earlier entreval (13c.) and directly from Late Latin intervallum "a space between, an interval of time, a distance," originally "space between palisades or ramparts" [OED], from inter "between" (see inter-) + vallum "rampart, palisade, wall," which is apparently a collective form of vallus "stake," from PIE *walso- "a post" (see wall (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 出/从 + 强壮/价值 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1831, back-formation from evaluation, or else from French évaluer, back-formation from évaluation.

meanings.definitionCn: 机械；机器；机构；机械装置

root.meaning: to make or form on a machine

morphology.mnemonic: to make or form on a machine + …的场所/与…有关

etymology.story: 1680s; from machine (n.) + -ery.

meanings.definitionCn: 地位；状态；情形；重要身份

etymology.story: 1670s, "height" of a situation or condition, later "legal standing of a person" (1791), from Latin status "condition, position, state, manner, attitude" from past participle stem of stare "to stand," from PIE *ste-tu-, from root *stā- "to stand" (see stet).
Old English gescildan, from the root of shield (n.).

1590s, "action of inferring;" 1610s, "that which is inferred;" from Medieval Latin inferentia, from Latin inferentem (nominative inferens), present participle of inferre "bring into; conclude, deduce" (see infer)..

meanings.definitionCn: 略读；撇去…的浮物；从…表面飞掠而过；去除；（为逃税而）隐瞒（部分收入）
撇；撇去的东西；表层物；瞒报所得的收入
脱脂的；撇去浮沫的；表层的

etymology.story: 1570s, "fact, occurrence," from Late Latin phænomenon, from Greek phainomenon "that which appears or is seen," noun use of neuter present participle of phainesthai "to appear," passive of phainein (see phantasm).

morphology.mnemonic: notice, attention, reputation + 使…化/造成

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French notefiier "make known, inform, apprise" (13c.), from Latin notificare "to make known, notify," from Latin notus "known" (see notice (n.)) + root of facere "make, do" (see factitious).

meanings.definitionCn: 提到，谈到；提及，论及；说起
洗衣店，洗衣房；要洗的衣服；洗熨；洗好的衣服

etymology.story: late 14c., "place for washing;" mid-15c., "act of washing," a contraction (compare launder) of Middle English lavendrie (late 13c.), from Old French lavanderie "wash-house," from Vulgar Latin lavandaria "things to be washed," plural of lavandarium, from lavare "to wash" (see lave).

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + 堆积/积累 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1520s, from Latin accumulatus, past participle of accumulare "to heap up" (see accumulation).

mid-13c., "sinfulness," from Old French crimne (12c., Modern French crime), from Latin crimen (genitive criminis) "charge, indictment, accusation; crime, fault, offense," perhaps from cernere "to decide, to sift" (see crisis).

meanings.definitionCn: 举起；使起伏；投掷；恶心；发出（叹息等）
举起；起伏；投掷；一阵呕吐

etymology.story: early 14c., leisir, "opportunity to do something," also "free time, time at one's disposal," from Old French leisir, loisir "capacity, ability, freedom (to do something); permission; spare time; free will; idleness, inactivity," noun use of infinitive leisir "be permitted," from Latin licere "to be allowed" (see licence (n.)).

late 13c., "to bring up, nurture" (a child, a feeling, etc.), from Old French norriss-, stem of norrir "raise, bring up, nurture, foster; maintain, provide for" (12c., Modern French nourrir), from Latin nutrire "to feed, nurse, foster, support, preserve," from *nutri (older form of nutrix "nurse"), literally "she who gives suck," from PIE *nu- (from root *(s)nau- "to swim, flow, let flow," hence "to suckle;" see nutriment) + fem.

early 15c., "to flood, overflow," from Anglo-French surounder, Middle French soronder "to overflow, abound; surpass, dominate," from Late Latin superundare "overflow," from Latin super "over" (see super-) + undare "to flow in waves," from unda "wave" (see water (n.1); and compare abound).

meanings.definitionCn: 香烟， 纸烟， 卷烟

etymology.story: 1835, American English, from French cigarette (by 1824), diminutive of cigare "cigar" (18c.), from Spanish cigarro (see cigar).

meanings.definitionCn: 火把，火炬；手电筒；启发之物
肯定的,积极的,绝对的,无疑的,正的
特别喜欢的， 中意的

etymology.story: 1837, from French menu de repas "list of what is served at a meal," from Middle French menu (adj.) "small, detailed" (11c.), from Latin minutus "small," literally "made smaller," past participle of minuere "to diminish," from root of minus "to diminish" (see minus).

meanings.definitionCn: [雷达] 雷达，无线电探测器

etymology.story: "electronic system for locating objects by means of radio waves," 1941, acronym (more or less) from radio detecting and ranging.

meanings.definitionCn: 难民，避难者；流亡者，逃亡者

etymology.story: 1680s, from French refugié, noun use of past participle of refugier "to take shelter, protect," from Old French refuge (see refuge).
early 15c., from Middle French comparable, from Latin comparabilis "capable of comparison," from comparare (see comparison).
1610s, "to indicate specially," from special (adj.) + -ize, perhaps on model of French spécialiser.
1794, intransitive, back-formation from rotation.

"a drama sung" [Klein], 1640s, from Italian opera, literally "a work, labor, composition," from Latin opera "work, effort" (Latin plural regarded as feminine singular), secondary (abstract) noun from operari "to work," from opus (genitive operis) "a work" (see opus).

morphology.mnemonic: 在…之前/预先 + sume + able + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: 1640s, "with presumption, without examination," from presumable + -ly (2).

early 13c., from Old French conscience "conscience, innermost thoughts, desires, intentions; feelings" (12c.), from Latin conscientia "knowledge within oneself, sense of right, a moral sense," from conscientem (nominative consciens), present participle of conscire "be (mutually) aware," from com- "with," or "thoroughly" (see com-) + scire "to know" (see science).
early 14c., from Old French suget, subject (Modern French sujet), from Latin subiectus (see subject (n.))..

morphology.mnemonic: 实践/做 + ic + 与…有关的/形容词 + 以…方式/副词化

etymology.story: 1620s, "in a practical manner," from practical + -ly (2).

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + 知道/认识 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: mid-15c., "knowledge of an event or incident; understanding," from Middle French recognition (15c.) and directly from Latin recognitionem (nominative recognitio) "a reviewing, investigation, examination," noun of action from past participle stem of recognoscere "to acknowledge, know again; examine" (see recognize).

meanings.definitionCn: 盒式磁带；暗盒；珠宝箱；片匣

etymology.story: 1793, "little box," from French cassette, from Middle French casset, diminutive of Old North French casse "box" (see case (n.2)).

Old English blæd "a leaf," but also "a leaf-like part" (of spade, oar, etc.), from Proto-Germanic *bladaz (source also of Old Frisian bled "leaf," German Blatt, Old Saxon, Danish, Dutch blad, Old Norse blað), from PIE *bhle-to-, suffixed form (past participle) of *bhel- (3) "to thrive, bloom," possibly identical with *bhel- (2) "to blow, swell" (see bole).

morphology.mnemonic: 统治 + 结果/过程/状态

etymology.story: 1727, from French arrangement, from arranger "arrange" (see arrange)..
early 15c., "income from property or possessions," from Middle French revenue, in Old French, "a return," noun use of fem.
"long, narrow, flat piece," mid-15c., "narrow piece of cloth," probably related to or from Middle Low German strippe "strap, thong," and from the same source as stripe (n.1).

meanings.definitionCn: 解答,解决办法;溶解,溶液

morphology.mnemonic: 松开/解决 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., "a solving or being solved," from Old French solucion "division, dissolving; explanation; payment" or directly from Latin solutionem (nominative solutio) "a loosening or unfastening," noun of action from past participle stem of solvere "to loosen, untie, solve, dissolve" (see solve).

morphology.mnemonic: to hang together + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: 1640s, from French continueus or directly from Latin continuus "uninterrupted, hanging together" (see continue).

1580s, from Latin opponentem (nominative opponens), present participle of opponere "oppose, object to," literally "set against, set opposite," from ob "against" (see ob-) + ponere "to put, set, place" (see position (n.))..

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + celer + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1520s, from Latin acceleratus, past participle of accelerare "to hasten,  quicken," from ad- "to" (see ad-) + celerare "hasten," from celer "swift" (see celerity).

meanings.definitionCn: 向后的；反向的；发展迟缓的

morphology.mnemonic: back + to parry, to fend off

meanings.definitionCn: 粮食；颗粒；[作物] 谷物；纹理

etymology.story: early 14c., "a small, hard seed," especially of one of the cereal plants, also as a collective singular, "seed of wheat and allied grasses used as food;" also "something resembling grain; a hard particle of other substances" (salt, sand, later gunpowder, etc.), from Old French grain, grein (12c.) "seed, grain; particle, drop; berry; grain as a unit of weight," from Latin granum "seed, a grain, small kernel," from PIE root *gre-no- "grain" (see corn (n.1)).
late 12c., "a virgin, a young unmarried woman," shortening of maiden (n.).

late 14c., "act of dwelling; dwelling place," from Old French residence, from Medieval Latin residentia, from Latin residentem (nominative residens) "residing, dwelling," present participle of residere "reside" (see reside).

meanings.definitionCn: 信用，信誉；[金融] 贷款；学分；信任；声望
相信，信任；把…归给，归功于；赞颂
不管的；不顾的；不注意的
不顾后果地；不管怎样，无论如何；不惜费用地

morphology.mnemonic: look upon, observe + 没有…的/无…的

etymology.story: "indifferent," 1590s, from regard (n.) + -less.

meanings.definitionCn: 加强的；集中的；透彻的；加强语气的

etymology.story: mid-15c., "intense, fervent, great," from Old French intensif (14c.) and Medieval Latin intensivus, from Latin intens-, past participle stem of intendere "turn one's attention; strain, stretch" (see intend).

meanings.definitionCn: (Alter)人名；(英)奥尔特；(德、捷、葡、爱沙、立陶、拉脱、俄、西、罗、瑞典)阿尔特

etymology.story: late 14c., "to change (something)," from Old French alterer "change, alter," from Medieval Latin alterare "to change," from Latin alter "the other (of the two)," from PIE *al- "beyond" (see alias (adv.)) + comparative suffix -ter (as in other).

meanings.definitionCn: 理想的；完美的；想象的；不切实际的

etymology.story: "carrying of oneself, deportment," mid-13c., verbal noun from bear (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 举例说明,阐明;图解,加插图

etymology.story: 1520s, "light up, shed light on;" 1610s, "educate by means of examples," back-formation from illustration, and in some cases from Latin illustratus, past participle of illustrare "light up, make light, illuminate." Sense of "provide pictures to explain or decorate" is 1630s.

meanings.definitionCn: 发生,出现;存在;想起,想到

etymology.story: 1520s, "meet, meet in argument," from Middle French occurrer "happen unexpectedly" or directly from Latin occurrere "run to meet, run against, befall, present itself," from ob "against, toward" (see ob-) + currere "to run" (see current (adj.)).

late 14c., from Old French comedie (14c., "a poem," not in the theatrical sense), from Latin comoedia, from Greek komoidia "a comedy, amusing spectacle," probably from komodios "actor or singer in the revels," from komos "revel, carousal, merry-making, festival," + aoidos "singer, poet," from aeidein "to sing," related to oide (see ode).

Old English brom "broom, brushwood," the common flowering shrub whose twigs were tied together to make a tool for sweeping, from Proto-Germanic *bræmaz "thorny bush" (source also of Dutch braam, German Brombeere "blackberry"), from PIE root *bh(e)rem- "to project, a point."

Traditionally, both the flowers and sweeping with broom twigs were considered unlucky in May (Suffolk, Sussex, Wiltshire, etc.).
late 15c., "settled arrangement," also "income, property," from establish + -ment.

morphology.mnemonic: device for pressing cloth + 行为/过程/结果

meanings.definitionCn: 微风；轻而易举的事；煤屑；焦炭渣；小风波

etymology.story: 1852 (originally in the medical sense and much scorned by the GPs); see special (adj.) + -ist.

in logic, "to 'bring in' as a conclusion of a process of reasoning," 1520s, from Latin inferre "bring into, carry in; deduce, infer, conclude, draw an inference; bring against," from in- "in" (see in- (2)) + ferre "carry, bear," from PIE *bher- (1) "to bear, to carry, to take" (source also of Sanskrit bharati "carries;" Avestan baraiti "carries;" Old Persian barantiy "they carry;" Armenian berem "I carry;" Greek pherein "to carry;" Old Irish beru/berim "I catch, I bring forth;" Gothic bairan "to carry;" Old English and Old High German beran, Old Norse bera "barrow;" Old Church Slavonic birati "to take;" Russian brat' "to take," bremya "a burden").

meanings.definitionCn: 背景的；发布背景材料的

etymology.story: 1670s, from back (adj.) + ground (n.); original sense was theatrical, later applied to painting.

meanings.definitionCn: 疼痛的，痛心的；剧烈的，极度的；恼火的，发怒的；厉害的，迫切的

etymology.story: Old English sar "bodily pain or injury, wound; sickness, disease; state of pain or suffering," from root of sore (adj.).

late 14c., from Old French disposer (13c.) "arrange, order, control, regulate" (influenced in form by poser "to place"), from Latin disponere "put in order, arrange, distribute," from dis- "apart" (see dis-) + ponere "to put, place" (past participle positus; see position (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 灼热；色彩鲜艳；兴高采烈
窗帘,门帘;幕(布);结束； vt遮掩

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old English hleahtor "laughter; jubilation; derision," from Proto-Germanic *hlahtraz (source also of Old Norse hlatr, Danish latter, Old High German lahtar, German Gelächter); see laugh (v.)..

meanings.definitionCn: 宪法；体制；章程；构造；建立，组成；体格

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 建立/放置 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: mid-14c., "law, regulation, edict," from Old French constitucion (12c.) "constitution, establishment," and directly from Latin constitutionem (nominative constitutio) "act of settling, settled condition, anything arranged or settled upon, regulation, order, ordinance," from constitut-, past participle stem of constituere (see constitute).

morphology.mnemonic: 确定 + 使…化/造成 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: early 15c., "action of certifying," from French certificat, from Medieval Latin certificatum "thing certified," noun use of neuter past participle of certificare (see certify).

meanings.definitionCn: 混乱；食堂，伙食团；困境；脏乱的东西
酸的；发酵的；刺耳的；酸臭的；讨厌的

etymology.story: late 15c., "destroy, eradicate," from Latin deletus, past participle of delere "destroy, blot out, efface," from delevi, originally perfective tense of delinere "to daub, erase by smudging" (as of the wax on a writing table), from de- "from, away" (see de-) + linere "to smear, wipe" (see lime (n.1)).

root.meaning: be conscious inwardly of (one's state or condition) is from 1680s. Meaning

morphology.mnemonic: 非/无 + be conscious inwardly of (one's state or condition) is from 1680s. Meaning

etymology.story: 1610s, from non- + sense; perhaps influenced by French nonsens..

morphology.mnemonic: 穿越/转变 + 形状/形态

etymology.story: mid-14c., "change the form of" (transitive), from Old French transformer (14c.), from Latin transformare "change in shape, metamorphose," from trans- "across" (see trans-) + formare "to form" (see form (v.)).

morphology.mnemonic: lead + er + 身份/状态/技能

etymology.story: 1821, "position of a leader, command," from leader + -ship.

1590s, "natural, not acquired," from Latin genuinus "native, natural, innate," from root of gignere "beget" (see genus), perhaps influenced in form by contrasting adulterinus "spurious." [Alternative etymology is from Latin genu "knee," from a supposed ancient custom of a father acknowledging paternity of a newborn by placing it on his knee.] Meaning "really proceeding from its reputed source" is from 1660s.

etymology.story: 1510s, "an interpreter," 1530s in the sense of "person who speaks for another or others." Irregular formation from spoke, past tense of speak (actually a back-formation from spoken) + man (n.).

morphology.mnemonic: equi + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., a term in astrology (from French équation, 14c.); general sense of "action of making equal" is from 1650s, from Latin aequationem (nominative aequatio) "an equal distribution, a sharing in common," noun of state from past participle stem of aequare (see equal (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 感人的；令人钦佩的；给人以深刻印象的

合成；混合；恶化，加重；和解，妥协

morphology.mnemonic: 建造 + 行为/过程/结果

"to become awake, cease to sleep," Old English wæcnan, wæcnian "to rise, awake; spring from, come into being," from the same source as wake (v.).

非常的;格外的;意外的;离奇的;临时的

etymology.story: early 15c., from Latin extraordinarius "out of the common order," from extra ordinem "out of order," especially the usual order, from extra "out" (see extra-) + ordinem, accusative of ordo "order" (see order (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 涉及；使关心（concern的ing形式）；忧虑
爱慕， 吸引； 吸引力； 具有吸引力的事

etymology.story: late 14c., from French attraction, from Latin attractionem (nominative attractio) "a drawing together," noun of action from past participle stem of attrahere (see attract).
late 15c., "of the nature of an agreement," from Late Latin conventionalis "pertaining to convention or agreement," from Latin conventionem (see convention).

morphology.mnemonic: regul + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1670s, "act of regulating; state of being reduced to order," noun of action from regulate.

循环， 流通； 流传， 传播； 发行， 发行量

morphology.mnemonic: ring环，圆 + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: mid-15c., from Middle French circulation or directly from Latin circulationem (nominative circulatio), noun of action from past participle stem of circulare "to form a circle," from circulus "small ring" (see circle (n.)).

Old English mere "sea, ocean; lake, pool, pond, cistern," from Proto-Germanic *mari (source also of Old Norse marr, Old Saxon meri "sea," Middle Dutch maer, Dutch meer "lake, sea, pool," Old High German mari, German Meer "sea," Gothic marei "sea," mari-saiws "lake"), from PIE *mori- "sea" (source also of Latin mare, Old Church Slavonic morje, Russian more, Lithuanian mares, Old Irish muir, Welsh mor "sea," Gaulish Are-morici "people living near the sea")..

morphology.mnemonic: 统治 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: "room and provisions, lodging," c.

late 14c., from Old French evident and directly from Latin evidentem (nominative evidens) "perceptible, clear, obvious, apparent" from ex- "fully, out of" (see ex-) + videntem (nominative videns), present participle of videre "to see" (see vision)..

early 14c., "area outside a town or city," whether agricultural or residential but most frequently residential, from Old French suburbe "suburb of a town," from Latin suburbium "an outlying part of a city" (especially Rome), from sub "below, near" (see sub-) + urbs (genitive urbis) "city" (see urban).

meanings.definitionCn: 旅馆；门房；集会处；山林小屋

etymology.story: Old English þreat "crowd, troop," also "oppression, coercion, menace," related to þreotan "to trouble, weary," from Proto-Germanic *thrautam (source also of Dutch verdrieten, German verdrießen "to vex"), from PIE *treud- "to push, press squeeze" (source also of Latin trudere "to press, thrust," Old Church Slavonic trudu "oppression," Middle Irish trott "quarrel, conflict," Middle Welsh cythrud "torture, torment, afflict").
Old English writere "one who can write, clerk; one who produces books or literary compositions," agent noun from writan (see write (v.)).
1650s, from French simplifier "to make simpler" (15c.), from Medieval Latin simplificare "to simplify," from Latin simplex "simple" (see simplex) + root of facere "to make" (see factitious).

Old English breost "breast, bosom; mind, thought, disposition," from Proto-Germanic *breustam "breast" (source also of Old Saxon briost, Old Frisian briast, Old Norse brjost, Dutch borst, German brust, Gothic brusts), perhaps literally "swelling" and from PIE root *bhreus- "to swell, sprout" (source also of Middle Irish bruasach "having a broad, strong chest," Old Irish bruinne "breast").

morphology.mnemonic: in + 来 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: early 15c., "finding or discovering of something," from Old French invencion (13c.) and directly from Latin inventionem (nominative inventio) "faculty of invention," noun of action from past participle stem of invenire "to come upon, find; find out; invent, discover, devise; ascertain; acquire, get earn," from in- "in, on" (see in- (2)) + venire "to come" (see venue).

late 12c., "God's forgiveness of his creatures' offenses," from Old French mercit, merci (9c.) "reward, gift; kindness, grace, pity," from Latin mercedem (nominative merces) "reward, wages, pay hire" (in Vulgar Latin "favor, pity"), from merx (genitive mercis) "wares, merchandise" (see market (n.)).

late 13c., from Anglo-French anuier, Old French enoiier, anuier "to weary, vex, anger; be troublesome or irksome to," from Late Latin inodiare "make loathsome," from Latin (esse) in odio "(it is to me) hateful," ablative of odium "hatred" (see odium).
mid-15c., metathesis of crulle (c.

early 14c., "to train or instruct in some specific subject," from Old French informer, enformer "instruct, teach" (13c.) and directly from Latin informare "to shape, give form to, delineate," figuratively "train, instruct, educate," from in- "into" (see in- (2)) + formare "to form, shape," from forma "form" (see form (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 心理/精神 + ic + 与…有关的/形容词

Meaning extended in ancient times by metaphor to "a defining quality."Meaning "sum of qualities that define a person" is from 1640s.

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 给予/分配 + 状态/行为/过程

1886, "devotee of the teachings of Marx," from French marxiste, from Karl Marx (1818-1883), German political theorist.

meanings.definitionCn: 直接地；砰然地；突然巨响地

early 14c., browes, brues "brow, forehead, eyebrow," earlier brouwes (c.
"sense, import, intent," c.

late 14c., "lacking wisdom or knowledge; unaware," from Old French ignorant (14c.), from Latin ignorantem (nominative ignorans) "not knowing, ignorant," present participle of ignorare "not to know, to be unacquainted; mistake, misunderstand; take no notice of, pay no attention to," from assimilated form of in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + Old Latin gnarus "aware, acquainted with" (source also of Classical Latin noscere "to know," notus "known"), from Proto-Latin suffixed form *gno-ro-, suffixed form of PIE root *gno- "to know" (see know).

1670s, from the phrase to come short "be inadequate" (1570s); see short (adj.).

meanings.definitionCn: 粗鲁的；无礼的；狂暴的；未开化的

etymology.story:
late 13c., "coarse, rough" (of surfaces), from Old French ruide (13c.) or directly from Latin rudis "rough, crude, unlearned," a word of uncertain etymology, related to rudus "rubble." The usual preferred derivation is that it is from the same source as Latin rufus "red" (see rufous) via a notion of raw ("red") meat, but de Vaan points out "there is not a shimmer of a meaning 'red' in rudis or in rudus 'rubble', so that the supposed shift from 'crude (meat)' > 'crude' rests in the air."

Sense of "ill-mannered, uncultured; uneducated, uncultured" is from mid-14c.

meanings.definitionCn: (疾病的)症状；(不好事情的)征兆,表征

Old English þærbig "thus, by means of or because of that;" see there + by.

Singular and plural used interchangeably in English until c.
1815, literal and figurative, American English, of unknown origin.
also fire-man, late 14c., "tender of a fire," from fire (n.) + man (n.).

late 14c., from Old French male, masle "male, masculine; a male" (see male (n.)).

"act of setting out on a journey, business, etc." 1759, from out + set (v.).

late 13c., "article of value used for adornment," from Anglo-French juel, Old French jouel "ornament; present; gem, jewel" (12c.), which is perhaps [Watkins] from Medieval Latin jocale, from Latin jocus "pastime, sport," in Vulgar Latin "that which causes joy" (see joke (n.)).

etymology.story: Old English wull "wool, fine soft hair which forms the coat of some animals," from Proto-Germanic *wulno (source also of Old Norse ull, Old Frisian wolle, Middle Dutch wolle, Dutch wol, Old High German wolla, German wolle, Gothic wulla), from PIE *wele- (1) "wool" (source also of Sanskrit urna; Avestan varena; Greek lenos "wool;" Latin lana "wool," vellus "fleece;" Old Church Slavonic vluna, Russian vulna, Lithuanian vilna "wool;" Middle Irish olann, Welsh gwlan "wool").
mid-14c., from whole (adj.) + -ly (2), or a modification of unrecorded Old English *hallice..

early 15c., "a prompting" (a sense now obsolete), from Old French instinct (14c.) or directly from Latin instinctus "instigation, impulse, inspiration," noun use of past participle of instinguere "to incite, impel," from in- "into, in, on, upon" (see in- (2)) + stinguere "prick, goad," from PIE *steig- "to prick, stick, pierce" (see stick (v.)).

late 14c., from Old French diametre, from Latin diametrus, from Greek diametros (gramme) "diagonal of a circle," from dia- "across, through" (see dia-) + metron "a measure" (see meter (n.2))..

Old English bereærn "barn," literally "barley house," from bere "barley" (see barley) + aern "house," metathesized from *rann, *rasn (source also of Old Norse rann, Gothic razn "house," Old English rest "resting place").
in reference to a dog sound, Old English beorcan "to bark," from Proto-Germanic *berkan (source also of Old Norse berkja "to bark"), of echoic origin.
mid-14c., from stem of Old French restreindre "press, push together; curb, bridle; bandage" (12c.), from Latin restringere "draw back tightly, confine, check" (see restriction).

Old English grædig (West Saxon), gredig (Anglian) "voracious, hungry," also "covetous, eager to obtain," from Proto-Germanic *grædagaz (source also of Old Saxon gradag "greedy," Old Norse graðr "greed, hunger," Danish graadig, Dutch gretig, Old High German gratag "greedy," Gothic gredags "hungry"), from *græduz (source also of Gothic gredus "hunger," Old English grædum "eagerly"), possibly from PIE root *gher- (5) "to like, want" (source of Sanskrit grdh "to be greedy").
