meanings.definitionCn: 生死攸关的,重大的;生命的,生机的

etymology.story: late 14c., "of or manifesting life," from Latin vitalis "of or belonging to life," from vita "life," related to vivere "to live," from PIE root *gweie- (1) "to live" (see bio-).
early 15c., "armed encounter, battle," from Old French conflit and directly from Latin conflictus (see conflict (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 别了！（常含有永别或不容易再见面的意思）；再会！

etymology.story: expression at parting, late 14c., from Middle English faren wel, verbal phrase attested by c.

meanings.definitionCn: 允许，准许；承认；给予；(for)考虑到

etymology.story: early 14c., allouen, "to commend, praise; approve of, be pleased with; appreciate the value of;" also, "take into account or give credit for," also, in law and philosophy, "recognize, admit as valid" (a privilege, an excuse, a statement, etc.).

root.meaning: seek a job by submitting an application for one

morphology.mnemonic: seek a job by submitting an application for one + ance

etymology.story: 1560s, "action of putting into use," from apply + -ance.

meanings.definitionCn: 块，团；群众，民众；大量，众多；质量
群众的，民众的；大规模的，集中的

etymology.story: early 15c., from Latin detectus, past participle of detegere "uncover, expose," figuratively "discover, reveal, disclose," from de- "un-, off" (see de-) + tegere "to cover" (see stegosaurus).

meanings.definitionCn: 爱好者；业余爱好者；外行

etymology.story: 1784, "one who has a taste for (something)," from French amateur "lover of," from Latin amatorem (nominative amator) "lover," agent noun from amatus, past participle of amare "to love" (see Amy).
1570s, from poison (n.) + -ous.

early 14c., condempner "to blame, censure," from Old French condamner "to condemn" (11c.), from Latin condemnare "to sentence, doom, blame, disapprove," from com-, intensive prefix (see com-), + damnare "to harm, damage" (see damn).

meanings.definitionCn: 兽， 牲畜 A；凶残的人； 举止粗鲁的人
协作; 援助; 帮助

etymology.story: early 15c., "act of helping or aiding," from Middle French assistance, from assister (see assist (v.))..
1530s, from Middle French commerce (14c.), from Latin commercium "trade, trafficking," from com- "together" (see com-) + merx (genitive mercis) "merchandise" (see market (n.))..
late 14c., "let in," from Latin admittere "to allow to enter, let in, let come, give access," from ad- "to" (see ad-) + mittere "let go, send" (see mission).
"cause to be interested, engage the attention of," c.

late 14c., "profound depth," from Old French golf "a gulf, whirlpool," from Italian golfo "a gulf, a bay," from Late Latin colfos, from Greek kolpos "bay, gulf of the sea," earlier "trough between waves, fold of a loose garment," originally "bosom," the common notion being "curved shape." This is from PIE *kwelp- "to arch, to vault" (compare Old English hwealf, a-hwielfan "to overwhelm").
early 14c., "a poet, a singer" (c.

meanings.definitionCn: 定义,解释;(轮廓影像等的)清晰度;阐明;

etymology.story: late 14c., "decision, setting of boundaries," from Old French definicion, from Latin definitionem (nominative definitio), noun of action from past participle stem of definire (see define).

meanings.definitionCn: 刮脸，剃胡子；修面；<口>侥幸逃过，幸免；剃刀，刮刀

etymology.story: Old English sceafan (strong verb, past tense scof, past participle scafen), "to scrape, shave, polish," from Proto-Germanic *skaban (source also of Old Norse skafa, Middle Dutch scaven, German schaben, Gothic skaban "scratch, shave, scrape"), from PIE *skabh-, collateral form of root *(s)kep- "to cut, to scrape, to hack" (see scabies).

meanings.definitionCn: [有化] 有机的；组织的；器官的；根本的

etymology.story: 1510s, "serving as an organ or instrument," from Latin organicus, from Greek organikos "of or pertaining to an organ, serving as instruments or engines," from organon "instrument" (see organ).

morphology.mnemonic: 拿/消耗 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: "a summary statement or account," c.

late 14c., "to put (one's faculties, etc.) to some task or career," late 14c., from Old French aploiier "apply, use, attach" (12c., Modern French appliquer), from Latin applicare "attach to, join, connect;" figuratively, "devote (oneself) to, give attention," from ad- "to" (see ad-) + plicare "fold" (see ply (v.1)).

meanings.definitionCn: 差的；自卑的；下级的，下等的

etymology.story: 1814 in the cookery sense, agent noun from steam (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 忽略；俯瞰；远眺；检查；高耸于…之上

morphology.mnemonic: 过度/超越 + appearance of a person

etymology.story: mid-14c., "to examine, scrutinize, inspect," from over- + look (v.).
1580s, "person skilled in politics," from politics + -ian.

early 15c., "unbelievable, surpassing belief as to what is possible," from Latin incredibilis "not to be believed, extraordinary," from in- "not" (see in- (1)) + credibilis "worthy of belief" (see credible).

meanings.definitionCn: 戏剧的；急剧的；引人注目的；激动人心的

etymology.story: 1580s, from Late Latin dramaticus, from Greek dramatikos "pertaining to plays," from drama (genitive dramatos; see drama).

1580s, from Spanish mosquito "little gnat," diminutive of mosca "fly," from Latin musca "fly," from PIE root *mu- "gnat, fly," imitative of insect buzzing (compare Sanskrit maksa-, Greek myia, Old English mycg, Modern English midge, Old Church Slavonic mucha), perhaps imitative of the sound of humming insects..

lute-like musical instrument, 1620s, from French guitare, which was altered by Spanish and Provençal forms from Old French guiterre, earlier guiterne, from Latin cithara, from Greek kithara "cithara," a triangular seven-stringed musical instrument related to the lyre, perhaps from Persian sihtar (see sitar).
also lay-out, "configuration, arrangement," 1852, from the verbal phrase; see lay (v.) + out (adv.).

meanings.definitionCn: 人工的，人造的，人为的；虚伪的，做作的

morphology.mnemonic: device, trick + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: late 14c., in the phrase artificial day "part of the day from sunrise to sunset," from Old French artificial, from Latin artificialis "of or belonging to art," from artificium (see artifice).

meanings.definitionCn: 虚构的，假想的；想像的；虚数的

etymology.story: "not real, existing only in fancy," late 14c., ymaginaire, from imagine + -ary; or else from Late Latin imaginarius "seeming, fancied," also literal, "pertaining to an image," from Latin imaginari "picture to oneself." Imaginary friend (one who does not exist) attested by 1789..

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + act of moving + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1590s; see remove (v.) + -al (2)..

1630s, "words used in a ceremony or ritual" (earlier as a Latin word in English), from Latin formula "form, draft, contract, regulation;" in law, "a rule, method;" literally "small form," diminutive of forma "form" (see form (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 注释； 评论； 意见

etymology.story: early 15c., from Middle French commenter (15c.), from Latin commentari, from commentum (see comment (n.)).
1550s, from Middle French révolte (c.

Old English stemn, stefn "stem of a plant, trunk of a tree," also "either end-post of a ship," from Proto-Germanic *stamniz (source also of Old Saxon stamm, Old Norse stafn "stem of a ship;" Danish stamme, Swedish stam "trunk of a tree;" Old High German stam, German Stamm), from suffixed form of PIE root *stā- "to stand" (see stet).
mid-13c., from sun (n.) + shine (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 成就， 成绩； 达到， 完成， 实现

morphology.mnemonic: to come to a head, + 结果/过程/状态

etymology.story: late 15c., "act of completing" (something), from Middle French achèvement "a finishing," noun of action from Old French achever "to finish, accomplish" (see achieve).
"manual laborer," late 14c., from Latin mechanicus, from Greek mekhanikos "an engineer," noun use of adjective meaning "full of resources, inventive, ingenious" (see mechanic (adj.)).
late 14c., from Old French desirable (12c.), from dasirer (see desire (v.)).

1610s, from Italian balcone, from balco "scaffold," from a Germanic source (perhaps Langobardic *balko- "beam," cognate with Old English balca "beam, ridge;" see balk (n.)) + Italian augmentative suffix -one.

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 站立/设置

etymology.story: 1520s, from Middle French consister (14c.) or directly from Latin consistere "to stand firm, take a standing position, stop, halt," from com- "together" (see com-) + sistere "to place," causative of stare "to stand, be standing" (see stay (v.)).

late 14c., "act of preparing," from Latin praeparationem (nominative praeparatio) "a making ready," noun of action from past participle stem of praeparare "prepare," from prae "before" (see pre-) + parare "make ready" (see pare).
1560s, raliabill, Scottish; see rely + -able.

1520s, "to recount, tell," from Middle French relater "refer, report" (14c.) and directly from Latin relatus, used as past participle of referre "bring back, bear back" (see refer), from re- "back, again" + latus (see oblate (n.)).

1580s, "one who passes judgment," from Middle French critique (14c.), from Latin criticus "a judge, literary critic," from Greek kritikos "able to make judgments," from krinein "to separate, decide" (see crisis).
1640s, originally of muscles which raise a part of the body, from Latin elevator "one who raises up," agent noun from past participle stem of elevare (see elevate).

late 14c., "Latin grammar, rules of Latin," from Old French gramaire "grammar; learning," especially Latin and philology, also "(magic) incantation, spells, mumbo-jumbo" (12c., Modern French grammaire), an "irregular semi-popular adoption" [OED] of Latin grammatica "grammar, philology," perhaps via an unrecorded Medieval Latin form *grammaria.

meanings.definitionCn: 使能够，使成为可能；授予权利或方法

morphology.mnemonic: 使成为/放入 + 有能力的，能干的

etymology.story: early 15c., "to make fit;" mid-15c., "to make able to," from en- (1) "make, put in" + able.

morphology.mnemonic: 在…之前/预先 + limin + …的场所/与…有关

etymology.story: 1660s, from French préliminaire and directly from Medieval Latin praeliminaris, from Latin prae- "before" (see pre-) + limen (genitive liminis) "threshold" (see limit (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 依靠的；从属的；取决于…的

morphology.mnemonic: 向下/移除/取消 + 悬挂/称量/支付 + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: 15c., variant spelling of dependant, now mostly restricted to adjectival use; see -ance.

mid-14c., from Old French obstacle, ostacle "opposition, obstruction, hindrance" (13c.) or directly from Latin obstaculum "a hindrance, obstacle," with instrumental suffix *-tlom + obstare "stand before, stand opposite to, block, hinder, thwart," from ob "against" (see ob-) + stare "to stand," from PIE root *stā- "to stand" (see stet).

meanings.definitionCn: 职业，专业；声明，宣布，表白

morphology.mnemonic: declare openly + 状态/行为/过程
计算 + 使…/具有…的 + …者/…器

etymology.story: late 14c., "mathematician, one who calculates," from Latin calculator, from calculatus, past participle of calculare "to reckon, compute," from calculus (see calculus).

meanings.definitionCn: 有篷汽车;有篷货运车厢

morphology.mnemonic: 生命/生物 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: 1819, from Greek bios "life" (see bio-) + -logy.

meanings.definitionCn: 虫，蠕虫；蜗杆；螺纹；小人物
使蠕动；给除虫；使缓慢前进

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + device for pressing cloth
向/朝/在 + a piece of rising ground

etymology.story: Old English arisan "to get up, rise; spring from, originate; spring up, ascend" (cognate with Old Saxon arisan, Gothic urreisan), from a- (1) "of" + rise (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 合作的， 协作的， 协力完成的

etymology.story: early 14c., cherischen, from Old French cheriss-, present participle stem of chierir "to hold dear" (12c., Modern French chérir), from chier "dear," from Latin carus "dear, costly, beloved" (see whore).

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 建立/放置

etymology.story: mid-15c., verb use of adjective constitute, "made up, formed" (late 14c.), from Latin constitutus "arranged, settled," past participle adjective from constituere "to cause to stand, set up, fix, place, establish, set in order; form something new; resolve," of persons, "to appoint to an office," from com-, intensive prefix (see com-), + statuere "to set," from PIE root *stā- "to stand," with derivatives meaning "place or thing that is standing" (see stet).

mid-13c., "property," from Anglo-French catel "property" (Old North French catel, Old French chatel), from Medieval Latin capitale "property, stock," noun use of neuter of Latin adjective capitalis "principal, chief" (see capital (n.1)).

meanings.definitionCn: [心理] 想象力；空想；幻想物

etymology.story: "faculty of the mind which forms and manipulates images," mid-14c., ymaginacion, from Old French imaginacion "concept, mental picture; hallucination," from Latin imaginationem (nominative imaginatio) "imagination, a fancy," noun of action from past participle stem of imaginari (see imagine)..

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + 站立/设置 + ance

etymology.story: mid-14c., from Old French resistance, earlier resistence, from Late Latin resistentia, from present participle stem of Latin resistere "make a stand against, oppose" (see resist).

meanings.definitionCn: 在…的下面；在…的形式下；在…的支配下

etymology.story: Old English underneoðan, from under + neoðan "below" (see beneath)..

meanings.definitionCn: 助手，大副；配偶；同事；配对物

etymology.story: 1520s, "to overcome in argument," from Latin convincere "to overcome decisively," from com-, intensive prefix (see com-), + vincere "to conquer" (see victor).

early 13c., from Old French empereor "emperor, leader, ruler" (11c.; accusative; nominative emperere; Modern French empereur), from Latin imperiatorem (nominative imperiator) "commander, emperor," from past participle stem of imperare "to command" (see empire).

morphology.mnemonic: 向前/支持 + to grow long, lengthen,

etymology.story: early 15c., back-formation from prolongation or else from Old French prolonguer, porloignier (13c.), from Late Latin prolongare "to prolong, extend," from Latin pro- "forth" (see pro-) + longus "long" (adj.); see long (adj.).

meanings.definitionCn: 理论； 原理； 学说；见解， 看法

morphology.mnemonic: 神 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: 1590s, "conception, mental scheme," from Late Latin theoria (Jerome), from Greek theoria "contemplation, speculation; a looking at, viewing; a sight, show, spectacle, things looked at," from theorein "to consider, speculate, look at," from theoros "spectator," from thea "a view" (see theater) + horan "to see," possibly from PIE root *wer- (4) "to perceive" (see ward (n.)).
late 14c., from Old French destresser, from Vulgar Latin *districtiare (see distress (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: in + tern + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: early 15c., "extending toward the interior," from Medieval Latin internalis, from Latin internus "within, inward, internal," figuratively "domestic," expanded from pre-Latin *interos, *interus "on the inside, inward," from PIE *en-ter- (source also of Old Church Slavonic anter, Sanskrit antar "within, between," Old High German unter "between," and the "down" sense of Old English under); suffixed (comparative) form of *en "in" (see in (adv.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 延长；延期；扩大；伸展；电话分机

morphology.mnemonic: 出/向外/前任 + 伸展/趋向 + 状态/行为/过程
标记 + 行为/过程/结果

etymology.story: 1530s, a kind of document in Scottish law, from Middle French signature (16c.) or directly from Medieval Latin signatura "signature, a rescript," in classical Latin "the matrix of a seal," from signatus, past participle of signare "to mark with a stamp, sign" (see sign (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 陡峭的；不合理的；夸大的；急剧升降的

etymology.story: "to soak in a liquid," early 14c., of uncertain origin, originally in reference to barley or malt, probably cognate with Old Norse steypa "to pour out, throw" (perhaps from an unrecorded Old English cognate), from Proto-Germanic *staupijanan.

meanings.definitionCn: 翻阅；以拇指拨弄；作搭车手势；笨拙地摆弄
共和国的；共和政体的；共和主义的；拥护共和政体的

etymology.story: "one who favors a republic or republican principles" (or, as Johnson puts it, "One who thinks a commonwealth without monarchy the best government"), 1690s; see republican (adj.).

meanings.definitionCn: 文学的；书面的；精通文学的

etymology.story: 1640s, "pertaining to alphabet letters," from French littéraire, from Latin literarius/litterarius "belonging to letters or learning," from littera/litera "letter" (see letter (n.1)).

meanings.definitionCn: 建筑师；设计师；缔造者；创造者

etymology.story: 1550s, from Middle French architecte, from Latin architectus, from Greek arkhitekton "master builder, director of works," from arkhi- "chief" (see archon) + tekton "builder, carpenter" (see texture).
Middle English forhed, from Old English forheafod "forehead, brow," from fore- + heafod (see head (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 古典的；经典的；传统的；第一流的

morphology.mnemonic: class + ic + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1590s, "of the highest rank" (originally in literature), from classic + -al (1).

meanings.definitionCn: 基础；地基；基金会；根据；创立

morphology.mnemonic: 基础 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., "action of founding," from Old French fondacion "foundation" (14c.) or directly from Late Latin fundationem (nominative fundatio) "a founding," noun of action from past participle stem of Latin fundare "to lay a bottom or foundation" (see found (v.1)).

"one who acts in place of another," early 15c., from Middle French substitut (noun use) and directly from Latin substitutus, past participle of substituere "put in place of another" (see substitution).

"to thrust, push; jostle;" also, "prod, to drive (cattle, etc.) by poking and prodding," late 14c., from Old French ponchonner "to punch, prick, stamp," from ponchon "pointed tool, piercing weapon" (see punch (n.1)).

meanings.definitionCn: 当然， 自然； 天生地

morphology.mnemonic: birth, + 与…有关的/形容词 + 以…方式/副词化

etymology.story: late 13c., "inherently, intrinsically, characteristically," from natural + -ly (2).

meanings.definitionCn: 柔韧， 有弹性； 柔顺， 温顺； 灵活性； 适应性

etymology.story: 1610s, of physical things, from French flexibilité (in Old French, "weakness, vacillation") or directly from Late Latin flexibilitatem (nominative flexibilitas), from Latin flexibilis "pliant, yielding" (see flexible).

meanings.definitionCn: 神秘的；不可思议的；难解的

morphology.mnemonic: myst + …的场所/与…有关 + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: 1610s, "full of mystery," from Latin mysterium (see mystery (n.1)) + -ous.

meanings.definitionCn: 再者， 加之， 而且

etymology.story: late 14c., in phrase and yit more ouer "there is more to say;" from more (adv.) + over (adv.).

meanings.definitionCn: 身份；同一性，一致；特性；恒等式

morphology.mnemonic: in + stall + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: mid-15c., "action of installing," in reference to church offices or other positions, from Medieval Latin installationem (nominative installatio), noun of action from past participle stem of installare (see install).

meanings.definitionCn: 书写；作品；著作；[法] 笔迹

etymology.story: Old English writing "action of forming letters and characters," verbal noun from write (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 联邦的；同盟的；联邦政府的； 联邦制的

morphology.mnemonic: 联盟 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1640s, as a theological term (in reference to "covenants" between God and man), from French fédéral, an adjective formed from Latin foedus (genitive foederis) "covenant, league, treaty, alliance," from PIE *bhoid-es-, from root *bheidh- "to trust" (which also is the source of Latin fides "faith;" see faith).

morphology.mnemonic: 暴力/违反 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: early 15c., "to break" (an oath, etc.), from Latin violatus, past participle of violare "treat with violence, dishonor, outrage" (see violation).

early 14c., from Old French verifier "substantiate, find out the truth about" (14c.), from Medieval Latin verificare "make true," from Latin verus "true" (see very) + root of facere "to make" (see factitious)..

morphology.mnemonic: paid by the recipient + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: early 15c., from Middle French collectif, from Latin collectivus, from collectus (see collect).

meanings.definitionCn: 短途旅行,游览;离题;[物理]偏移,漂移

morphology.mnemonic: 出/向外/前任 + to swear profanely + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1570s, "a deviation in argument," also "a military sally," from Latin excursionem (nominative excursio) "a running forth, sally, excursion, expedition," figuratively "an outset, opening," noun of action from past participle stem of excurrere "run out, run forth, hasten forward; project, extend," from ex- "out" (see ex-) + currere "to run" (see current (adj.)).

"young ox," Old English steor "bullock," from Proto-Germanic *steuraz (source also of Old Saxon stior, Old Norse stjorr, Swedish tjur, Danish tyr, Middle Dutch, Dutch, German stier, Gothic stiur "bull"), perhaps from PIE *steu-ro-, denoting "larger domestic animal" (see taurus).

1570s, "the consequence of anything" (as in in the event that); 1580s, "that which happens;" from Middle French event, from Latin eventus "occurrence, accident, event, fortune, fate, lot, issue," from past participle stem of evenire "to come out, happen, result," from assimilated form of ex- "out" (see ex-) + venire "to come" (see venue).

meanings.definitionCn: 学院；研究院；学会；专科院校

etymology.story: late 15c., "the classical Academy," from French Académie, from Latin Academia, from Greek Akademeia "grove of Akademos," a legendary Athenian of the Trojan War tales (his name apparently means "of a silent district"), whose estate, six stadia from Athens, was the enclosure where Plato taught his school.
Old English undergan "obtain, get; undertake," from under + gan (see go (v.)).

early 15c., "to shut (someone or something) in materially, enclose, imprison, confine," also "to have (something) as a constituent part," from Latin includere "to shut in, enclose, imprison, insert," from in- "in" (see in- (2)) + claudere "to shut" (see close (v.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/一起 + 感情/疾病 + ize

etymology.story: "have fellow-feeling," c.
1707, from along + side (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 燕子;吞咽vt.吞,咽;轻信;忍受;vi.咽口水

etymology.story: "ingest through the throat" (transitive), Old English swelgan "swallow, imbibe, absorb" (class III strong verb; past tense swealg, past participle swolgen), from Proto-Germanic *swelgan/*swelhan (source also of Old Saxon farswelgan, Old Norse svelgja "to swallow," Middle Dutch swelghen, Dutch zwelgen "to gulp, swallow," Old High German swelahan "to swallow," German schwelgen "to revel"), probably from PIE root *swel- (1) "to eat, drink" (source also of Iranian *khvara- "eating").

meanings.definitionCn: 代理(处)；代办处；[美](政府的)机关，厅

etymology.story: 1650s, "active operation," from Medieval Latin agentia, noun of state from Latin agentem (nominative agens) "effective, powerful," present participle of agere "to drive, conduct" (see act (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 息票；赠券；联票；[经] 配给券

etymology.story: 1822, "certificate of interest due on a bond" (which could be cut from the bond and presented for payment), from French coupon, literally "piece cut off," from couper "to cut," from coup "a blow" (see coup).

meanings.definitionCn: 由铁道运输；铺设铁路；以捏造不实之罪使入狱

etymology.story: 1704, "kernel of a nut," 1708, "head of a comet," from Latin nucleus "kernel," from nucula "little nut," diminutive of nux (genitive nucis) "nut," from PIE *kneu- "nut" (source also of Middle Irish cnu, Welsh cneuen, Middle Breton knoen "nut," Old Norse hnot, Old English hnutu "nut").
"bewitching, charming," 1640s, present-participle adjective from fascinate).
1540s, from Latin abstractus or else from the adjective abstract.
Old English stincg, steng "act of stinging, puncture, thrust," from the root of sting (v.).
late 13c., from Old French haster "hurry, make haste; urge, hurry along" (Modern French hâter), from haste "haste, urgency" (see haste).
1650s, from Latin agenda, literally "things to be done," neuter plural of agendus, gerundive of agere "to do" (see act (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: seem, have a certain appearance + ance

etymology.story: late 14c., "visible state or form, figure; mere show," from Anglo-French apparaunce, Old French aparance "appearance, display, pomp" (13c.), from Latin apparentia, abstract noun from aparentem, past participle of apparere (see appear).

meanings.definitionCn: 液体的；清澈的；明亮的；易变的
人类能力的高度发展，文化，教养，文明

etymology.story: mid-15c., "the tilling of land," from Middle French culture and directly from Latin cultura "a cultivating, agriculture," figuratively "care, culture, an honoring," from past participle stem of colere "tend, guard, cultivate, till" (see colony).

meanings.definitionCn: [机] 套筒，[机] 套管；袖子，[服装] 袖套

etymology.story: Old English sliefe (West Saxon), slefe (Mercian) "arm-covering part of a garment," probably literally "that into which the arm slips," from Proto-Germanic *slaubjon (source also of Middle Low German sloven "to dress carelessly," Old High German sloufen "to put on or off").

meanings.definitionCn: 献身， 奉献； 忠实； 热爱； 虔诚

etymology.story: early 13c., from Old French devocion "devotion, piety," from Latin devotionem (nominative devotio), noun of action from past participle stem of devovere "dedicate by a vow, sacrifice oneself, promise solemnly," from de- "down, away" (see de-) + vovere "to vow," from votum "vow" (see vow (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 匿名的，无名的，姓氏不明的

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + 名/法则 + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: "commotion, disturbance, tumult," late 14c.
1520s, from fisher + man (n.)..

Old English spannan "to join, link, clasp, fasten, bind, connect; stretch, span," from Proto-Germanic *spannan (source also of Old Norse spenna, Old Frisian spanna, Middle Dutch spannen, Dutch spannan "stretch, bend, hoist, hitch," Old High German spannan, German spannen "to join, fasten, extend, connect"), from PIE root *(s)pen- "to draw, stretch, spin" (source also of Latin pendere "to hang, to cause to hang," pondus "weight" (perhaps the notion is the weight of a thing measured by how much it stretches a cord), pensare "to weigh, consider;" Greek ponos "toil," ponein "to toil;" Lithuanian spendziu "lay a snare;" Old Church Slavonic peti "stretch, strain," pato "fetter," pina "I span;" Old English spinnan "to spin;" for other cognates, see spin (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 存在，实在；生存，生活；存在物，实在物

etymology.story: late 14c., "reality," from Old French existence, from Medieval Latin existentia/exsistentia, from existentem/exsistentem (nominative existens/exsistens) "existent," present participle of Latin existere/exsistere "stand forth, come out, emerge; appear, be visible, come to light; arise, be produced; turn into," and, as a secondary meaning, "exist, be;" from ex- "forth" (see ex-) + sistere "cause to stand" (see assist)..
Old English cræftan "to exercise a craft, build," from the same source as craft (n.).
"head," early 14c., polle "hair of the head; piece of fur from the head of an animal," also "head," from Middle Low German or Middle Dutch pol "head, top." Sense extended early 14c.

meanings.definitionCn: 少数民族；少数派；未成年

root.meaning: secondary subject of study, subject of study with fewer credits than a major

morphology.mnemonic: secondary subject of study, subject of study with fewer credits than a major + ity

etymology.story: 1530s, "condition of being smaller," from Middle French minorité (15c.), or directly from Medieval Latin minoritatem (nominative minoritas), from Latin minor (see minor (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 彩车，花车；漂流物；浮舟；浮萍

etymology.story: apparently an early Middle English merger of three related Old English nouns, flota "boat, fleet," flote "troop, flock," flot "body of water, sea;" all from the source of float (v.).
mid-15c., "full of (divine) grace," also "pleasant, sweet," from grace (n.) + -ful.
Old English feowerfeald; see four + -fold.

meanings.definitionCn: 更好的，更可取的；更合意的

morphology.mnemonic: to esteem (something) more than others + able

etymology.story: 1640s, from or on model of French préférable, from préfér (see prefer).

meanings.definitionCn: 参考，参照；涉及，提及；参考书目；介绍信；证明书

root.meaning: to commit to some authority for a decision

morphology.mnemonic: to commit to some authority for a decision + ance

meanings.definitionCn: 青蛙；[铁路] 辙叉；饰扣
（身体或器官内的）室，膛；房间；会所
把…关在室内；装填（弹药等）
整理， 排列， 布置 A；安排， 筹备

etymology.story: late 14c., "draw up a line of battle," from Old French arengier (12c.), from a- "to" (see ad-) + rangier "set in a row" (Modern French ranger), from rang "rank," from Frankish *hring (see rank (n.)).
early 15c., cors "ordinary" (modern spelling is from late 16c.), probably adjectival use of noun cours (see course (n.)), originally referring to rough cloth for ordinary wear.

meanings.definitionCn: 宣传，宣扬；公开；广告；注意

etymology.story: 1791, "condition of being public," from French publicité (1690s), from Medieval Latin publicitatem (nominative publicitas), from Latin publicus (see public (adj.)).

mid-13c., "part from each other," from Old French departir (10c.) "to divide, distribute; separate (oneself), depart; die," from Late Latin departire "divide" (transitive), from de- "from" (see de-) + partire "to part, divide," from pars (genitive partis) "a part" (see part (n.)).

1530s, "to pile up, accumulate," from Latin exaggeratus, past participle of exaggerare "heighten, amplify, magnify," literally "to heap, pile, load, fill," from ex- "thoroughly" (see ex-) + aggerare "heap up, accumulate," figuratively "amplify, magnify," from agger (genitive aggeris) "heap," from aggerere "bring together, carry toward," from assimilated form of ad- "to, toward" (see ad-) + gerere "carry" (see gest).
late 14c., implien, emplien "to enfold, enwrap, entangle" (the classical Latin sense), from Old French emplier, from Latin implicare "involve, enfold, entangle" (see implication).

meanings.definitionCn: 委托； 许诺； 承担义务

etymology.story: 1610s, "action of officially consigning to the custody of the state," from commit + -ment.

morphology.mnemonic: 狂热 + 行为/过程/结果

meanings.definitionCn: 碰撞；冲突；（意见，看法）的抵触；（政党等的）倾轧

etymology.story: early 15c., from Middle French collision (15c.), from Latin collisionem (nominative collisio) "a dashing together," noun of action from collidere (see collide)..

meanings.definitionCn: 资格；条件；限制；赋予资格

morphology.mnemonic: to limit, modify + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1540s, "restriction, modification," from Middle French qualification and directly from Medieval Latin qualificationem (nominative qualificatio), noun of action from past participle stem of qualificare (see qualify).

meanings.definitionCn: 供应,供应品;预备;条款;(pl.)给养

morphology.mnemonic: 向前/支持 + 看见 + 状态/行为/过程

meanings.definitionCn: 补充；聘用；征募；使…恢复健康

etymology.story: early 13c., "character attributed to someone;" late 13c., "celebrity, renown," from Old French fame "fame, reputation, renown, rumor" (12c.), from Latin fama "talk, rumor, report; reputation, public opinion; renown, good reputation," but also "ill-fame, scandal, reproach," from PIE root *bha- (2) "to speak, tell, say" (source also of Sanskrit bhanati "speaks;" Latin fari "to say," fabula "narrative, account, tale, story;" Armenian ban, bay "word, term;" Old Church Slavonic bajati "to talk, tell;" Old English boian "to boast," ben "prayer, request;" Greek pheme "speech, voice, utterance, a speaking, talk," phone "voice, sound," phanai "to speak;" Old Irish bann "law").

meanings.definitionCn: 更换；复位；代替者；补充兵员

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + 取悦 + 结果/过程/状态

etymology.story: "act or fact of being replaced," 1790, from replace (v.) + -ment.

meanings.definitionCn: 泥；诽谤的话；无价值的东西

etymology.story: mid-14c., cognate with and probably from Middle Low German mudde, Middle Dutch modde "thick mud," from Proto-Germanic *mud- from PIE *(s)meu-/*mu- [Buck], found in many words denoting "wet" or "dirty" (source also of Greek mydos "damp, moisture," Old Irish muad "cloud," Polish muł "slime," Sanskrit mutra- "urine," Avestan muthra- "excrement, filth"); related to German Schmutz "dirt," which also is used for "mud" in roads, etc., to avoid dreck, which originally meant "excrement." Welsh mwd is from English.

morphology.mnemonic: 统治 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: late 14c., "act of coming to land at the end of a voyage by sea, disembarkation," from Anglo-French arrivaille, from Old French arriver "to come to land" (see arrive).

morphology.mnemonic: 知道 + …的人/处于…状态 + ic

etymology.story: 1580s, from Middle French scientifique, from Medieval Latin scientificus "pertaining to science," from Latin scientia "knowledge" (see science) + -ficus "making" + facere "to make" (see factitious).

meanings.definitionCn: 频繁的；时常发生的；惯常的

etymology.story: late 15c., "visit or associate with," from Old French frequenter "attend frequently; assemble, gather together," from Latin frequentare "visit regularly; do frequently, repeat; assemble in throngs," from frequentem (see frequent (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 相等的， 一致的， 依…而定的

etymology.story: mid-15c., insuren, spelling variant of ensuren "to assure, give formal assurance" (late 14c.), also "make secure, make safe" (c.

meanings.definitionCn: 粉；粉末；[化工][军] 火药；尘土

root.meaning: one who has been elected to parliament

morphology.mnemonic: one who has been elected to parliament + 身份/状态/技能

etymology.story: 1640s, "state of being a member," from member + -ship.

meanings.definitionCn: 错误， 过失； 误差

etymology.story: also, through 18c., errour; c.

meanings.definitionCn: 平台,台;站台,月台;政纲,党纲

etymology.story: 1540s, "plan of action, scheme, design," from Middle French plateforme, platte fourme, literally "flat form," from Old French plat "flat" (see plateau (n.)) + forme "form" (see form (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 妒忌的；猜疑的；唯恐失去的；戒备的
痕迹，踪迹；微量；[仪] 迹线；缰绳

etymology.story: late 14c., "follow (a course); draw a line, make an outline of something," also figurative; "ponder, investigate," from Old French tracier "look for, follow, pursue" (12c., Modern French tracer), from Vulgar Latin *tractiare "delineate, score, trace" (source also of Spanish trazar "to trace, devise, plan out," Italian tracciare "to follow by foot"), a frequentative form from Latin tractus "track, course," literally "a drawing out," from past participle stem of trahere "to pull, draw" (see tract (n.1)).

"member of a black-skinned race of Africa," 1550s, from Spanish or Portuguese negro "black," from Latin nigrum (nominative niger) "black, dark, sable, dusky," figuratively "gloomy, unlucky, bad, wicked," of unknown origin (perhaps from PIE *nekw-t- "night;" see Watkins).

"to engrave," Old English grafan "to dig, dig up; engrave, carve, chisel" (medial -f- pronounced as "v" in Old English; past tense grof, past participle grafen), from Proto-Germanic *grabanan (source also of Old Norse grafa "to dig; engrave; inquire into," Old Frisian greva, Dutch graven "to dig, delve," Old High German graban, German graben, Gothic graban "to dig, carve"), from the same source as grave (n.).

morphology.mnemonic: 判定/控告 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: 1890, from Latin stem of criminal + -ology.

meanings.definitionCn: 优点，长处，有利条件；利益，好处

etymology.story: early 14c., avantage, "position of being in advance of another," from Old French avantage "advantage, profit, superiority," from avant "before," probably via an unrecorded Late Latin *abantaticum, from Latin abante (see advance).
mid-15c., "helpful, of assistance," from Middle French assistent (see assistant (n.))..
Old English inn "lodging, dwelling, house," probably from inne (adv.) "inside, within" (see in).

meanings.definitionCn: 作为替代； 却， 反而

etymology.story: 1590s, contraction of Middle English prepositional phrase ine stede (early 13c.; see stead), itself a loan-translation of Latin in loco (French en lieu de).

late 14c., from Old French controversie or directly from Latin controversia, from controversus "turned in an opposite direction, disputed, turned against," from contra- "against" (see contra) + versus (see verse)..

meanings.definitionCn: 命令； 指挥， 统率
命令， 指令；统帅； 指挥；掌握， 运用能力

etymology.story: 1872, from news (n.) + stand (n.)..
1580s, from worth (n.) + -less.

meanings.definitionCn: 履行,执行;表演,演出;性能,特性;成绩

etymology.story: late a5c., "accomplishment" (of something), from perform + -ance.

meanings.definitionCn: 大使；特使，(派驻国际组织的)代表

etymology.story: late 14c., also embassador, from Middle French ambassadeur, from Old French embassator, via Provençal or Old Spanish from Latin ambactus "a servant, vassal," from Celtic amb(i)actos "a messenger, servant," from PIE *ambhi- "about" (see ambi-) + *ag- "drive, lead" (see act (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 预料；期望；预先考虑；抢先；提前使用

etymology.story: 1530s, "to cause to happen sooner," a back-formation from anticipation, or else from Latin anticipatus, past participle of anticipare "take (care of) ahead of time," literally "taking into possession beforehand," from ante "before" (see ante) + capere "to take" (see capable).

meanings.definitionCn: 变量的；可变的；易变的，多变的；变异的，[生物] 畸变的
[数] 变量；可变物，可变因素

etymology.story: 1550s, "stage in growing," from grow + -th (2), on model of health, stealth, etc.

meanings.definitionCn: 对…作景观美化， 美化

etymology.story: 1530s, from Middle French carrotte, from Latin carota, from Greek karoton "carrot," probably from PIE *kre-, from root *ker- (1) "horn, head" (see horn (n.)); so called for its horn-like shape.

morphology.mnemonic: 标准/规范 + 与…有关的/形容词 + 以…方式/副词化

etymology.story: 1590s, "regularly," from normal + -ly (2).

morphology.mnemonic: 出/向外/前任 + 取/抓 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., from Anglo-French excepcioun, Old French excepcion, from Latin exceptionem (nominative exceptio) "an exception, restriction, limitation; an objection," noun of action from past participle stem of excipere "to take out" (see except).

meanings.definitionCn: 不明确的,含糊的,暧昧的

etymology.story: "uncertain as to specifics," 1540s, from Middle French vague "empty, vacant; wild, uncultivated; wandering" (13c.), from Latin vagus "strolling, wandering, rambling," figuratively "vacillating, uncertain," of unknown origin.

"lasting only for a time," 1540s, from Latin temporarius "of seasonal character, lasting a short time," from tempus (genitive temporis) "time, season" (see temporal, late 14c., which was the earlier word for "lasting but for a time").

Old English faran "to journey, set forth, go, travel, wander, make one's way," also "be, happen, exist; be in a particular condition," from Proto-Germanic *faran "to go" (source also of Old Saxon, Old High German, Gothic faran, Old Norse and Old Frisian fara, Dutch varen, German fahren), from PIE *por- "going, passage," from root *per- (2) "to lead, pass over" (see port (n.1)).

meanings.definitionCn: 不顾一切的，铤而走险的；绝望的，危急的

etymology.story: early 15c., "despairing, hopeless," from Latin desperatus "given up, despaired of," past participle of desperare (see despair (v.)).
1590s, "that goes first," present participle adjective from lead (v.1).

meanings.definitionCn: 可怕的；极坏的；使人敬畏的

etymology.story: 1660s, from accurate + -cy..

meanings.definitionCn: 便利的；手边的，就近的；容易取得的；敏捷的

morphology.mnemonic: to pass (something to someone) + 具有…特征/名词化

meanings.definitionCn: 强烈的欲望，迫切要求；推动力

morphology.mnemonic: determined, decided, absolute, final + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., "a breaking into parts," from Old French resolution (14c.) or directly from Latin resolutionem (nominative resolutio) "process of reducing things into simpler forms," from past participle stem of resolvere "loosen" (see resolve).
1779, from Latin via "by way of," ablative form of via "way, road, path, highway, channel, course," from PIE *wegh- "to go, convey" (see weigh)..

meanings.definitionCn: 环境；安装；布置；[天] 沉落
放置；沉没；使…处于某位置（set的ing形式）

etymology.story: late 14c., "fact or action of setting (something), putting, placing, planting," verbal noun from set (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 获得；评价；划线，刻划；把…记下

etymology.story: "to cut with incisions or notches," c.

meanings.definitionCn: 大学毕业生,研究生v.大学毕业a.毕了业的

etymology.story: early 15c., "to confer a university degree upon," from Medieval Latin graduatus (see graduate (n.)).
mid-14c., from Old French famine "famine, starvation" (12c.), from Vulgar Latin *famina, from Latin fames "hunger, starvation, famine," which is of unknown origin..

meanings.definitionCn: 缺席的；缺乏的,不存在的;心不在焉的

etymology.story: "to keep away" (from), c.
"scold," 1739, of unknown origin; perhaps related to Danish dialectal rag "grudge."

morphology.mnemonic: 看见 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: early 15c., "pertaining to the faculty of sight;" also "coming from the eye or sight" (as a beam of light was thought to do), from Late Latin visualis "of sight," from Latin visus "a sight, a looking; power of sight; things seen, appearance," from visus, past participle of videre "to see" (see vision).

meanings.definitionCn: 了解的；聪明的；有理解力的

etymology.story: Old English understanding "comprehension," verbal noun from understand (v.).

early 14c., from Old French empire "rule, authority, kingdom, imperial rule" (11c.), from Latin imperium "a rule, a command; authority, control, power; supreme power, sole dominion; military authority; a dominion, realm," from imperare "to command," from assimilated form of in- "in" (see in- (2)) + parare "to order, prepare" (see pare).

mid-15c., "bring up (children), to train," from Latin educatus, past participle of educare "bring up, rear, educate" (source also of Italian educare, Spanish educar, French éduquer), which is a frequentative of or otherwise related to educere "bring out, lead forth," from ex- "out" (see ex-) + ducere "to lead" (see duke (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 惭愧的，感到难为情的；耻于……的

etymology.story: Old English asceamed "feeling shame, filled with shame," past participle of ascamian "to feel shame," from a- intensive prefix + scamian "be ashamed, blush; cause shame" (see shame (v.)).

early 15c., "to tell, inform, impart knowledge or information," also "furnish with authoritative directions," from Latin instructus, past participle of instruere "arrange, prepare, set in order; inform, teach," literally "to build, erect," from in- "on" (see in- (2)) + struere "to pile, build" (see structure (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 几乎， 差不多； 实际上， 事实上

etymology.story: early 15c., "as far as essential qualities or facts are concerned;" from virtual + -ly (2).

meanings.definitionCn: 相当地； 公平地； 简直， 完全地

etymology.story: also hand-writing, "writing with the hand; form of writing peculiar to a person," early 15c., from hand (n.) + writing, translating Latin manuscriptum and equivalent to Greek kheirographia.

meanings.definitionCn: 生的；未加工的；阴冷的；刺痛的；擦掉皮的；无经验的；（在艺术等方面）不成熟的

etymology.story: Old English hreaw "uncooked, raw," from Proto-Germanic *khrawaz (source also of Old Norse hrar, Danish raa, Old Saxon hra, Middle Dutch rau, Dutch rauw, Old High German hrawer, German roh), from PIE root *kreue- (1) "raw flesh" (source also of Sanskrit kravih "raw flesh," krura- "bloody, raw, hard;" Greek kreas "flesh;" Latin crudus "not cooked," cruor "thick blood;" Old Irish cru, Lithuanian kraujas, Old Church Slavonic kruvi "blood;" Old English hrot "thick fluid, serum").

morphology.mnemonic: 向前/支持 + portion + 与…有关的/形容词
经济/管理 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: 1821 as a term in advertising, at first meant simply "cheaper," then "bigger and thus cheaper per unit or amount" (1950).

meanings.definitionCn: 同一地区全体居民,社会,社区;共同体

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French comunité "community, commonness, everybody" (Modern French communauté), from Latin communitatem (nominative communitas) "community, society, fellowship, friendly intercourse; courtesy, condescension, affability," from communis "common, public, general, shared by all or many," (see common (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 数据包，信息包；小包，小捆

etymology.story: mid-15c., from Middle English pak "bundle" (see pack (n.)) + diminutive suffix -et; perhaps modeled on Anglo-French pacquet (Middle French pacquet), which ultimately is a diminutive of Middle Dutch pak.

1580s, "a translation," from Middle French version, from Medieval Latin versionem (nominative versio) "a turning, a translation," from past participle stem of Latin vertere "to turn, change, alter, translate" (see versus).
1919, from air (n.1) + port (n.1).
1550s, from court (n.) + yard (n.1)..

root.meaning: large group of people with common ancestry.

morphology.mnemonic: large group of people with common ancestry. + 与…有关的/形容词 + ity

etymology.story: 1690s, "national quality," from national + -ity (in some usages perhaps from French nationalité.
1620s, "act of selecting," from Latin selectionem (nominative selectio) "a choosing out, choice, selection," noun of action from past participle stem of seligere (see select (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 开始（调查）；制定；创立；提起（诉讼）

etymology.story: 1510s, "purpose, design," from Latin institutum "an ordinance; a purpose; a custom; precedents; principal components," literally "thing set up," noun use of neuter past participle of instituere "to set up, put in place; arrange; found, establish" (see institute (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 在飞机上；[船] 在船上；在火车上

etymology.story: late 14c., probably in most cases from Old French à bord, from à "on" + bord "board," from Frankish *bord or a similar Germanic source (see board (n.2)); the "boarding" or sides of a vessel extended to the ship itself.

late 14c., "to undergo or suffer" (especially without breaking); also "to continue in existence," from Old French endurer (12c.) "make hard, harden; bear, tolerate; keep up, maintain," from Latin indurare "make hard," in Late Latin "harden (the heart) against," from in- (see in- (2)) + durare "to harden," from durus "hard," from PIE *dru-ro-, from root *deru- "be firm, solid, steadfast" (see true).
1630s, from French détailler "cut up in pieces; narrate in particulars," from Old French detaillier, from detail (see detail (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: make short + 以…方式/副词化

etymology.story: Old English scortlice "briefly," also, in late Old English, "in short time;" from short (adj.) + -ly (2)..

meanings.definitionCn: 适当的恰当的特有的vt.拨给,挪用,盗用

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + propri + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: "specially suitable, proper," early 15c., from Latin appropriatus, past participle of appropriare (see appropriate (v.)).

Old English þrote (implied in þrotbolla "the Adam's apple, larynx," literally "throat boll"), related to þrutian "to swell," from Proto-Germanic *thrut- (source also of Old High German drozza, German Drossel, Old Saxon strota, Middle Dutch strote, Dutch strot "throat"), of uncertain origin.

late 14c., "fact of being useful," from Old French utilite "usefulness" (13c., Modern French + utilité), earlier utilitet (12c.), from Latin utilitatem (nominative utilitas) "usefulness, serviceableness, profit," from utilis "usable," from uti "make use of, profit by, take advantage of" (see use (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 立即， 马上； 紧接地； 直接地

etymology.story: "without intervening time or space, directly," early 15c., from immediate + -ly (2)..

early 15c., "service book used by a priest," from Old French manuel "handbook" (also "plow-handle"), from Late Latin manuale "case or cover of a book, handbook," noun use of neuter of Latin manualis (see manual (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 测量；[计量] 度量；尺寸；量度制

root.meaning: size or quantity as ascertained by measuring

morphology.mnemonic: size or quantity as ascertained by measuring + 结果/过程/状态

etymology.story: 1751, "act of measuring," from measure (v.) + -ment.
1845, from optimist + -ic.
1530s, "list of words with explanations," from Medieval Latin vocabularium "a list of words," from Latin vocabulum "word, name, noun," from vocare "to name, call" (see voice (n.)).
1530s, of armies, "to retreat," from Middle French retirer "to withdraw (something)," from re- "back" (see re-) + Old French tirer "to draw" (see tirade).

meanings.definitionCn: 样子, 外表, 面貌, (问题等的)方面

etymology.story: late 14c., an astrological term, "relative position of the planets as they appear from earth" (i.e., how they "look at" one another); later also "way of viewing things," from Latin aspectus "a seeing, looking at, sight, view, countenance, appearance," from past participle of aspicere "to look at," from ad- "to" (see ad-) + specere "to look" (see scope (n.1)).

meanings.definitionCn: 粒子,微粒;极小量;小品词,虚词

etymology.story: late 14c., "small part or division of a whole, minute portion of matter," from Latin particula "little bit or part, grain, jot," diminutive of pars (genitive partis) "part;" see part (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 侵略的，好斗的，有进取心的，敢作敢为的

etymology.story: 1791, from Latin aggress-, past participle stem of aggredi "to approach, attack" (see aggression) + -ive.

meanings.definitionCn: 行为， 举止；特性； 运转情况 A

etymology.story: late 15c., essentially from behave, but with ending from Middle English havour "possession," a word altered (by influence of have) from aver, noun use of Old French verb aveir "to have.".
late 14c., "one skilled in music," from Old French musicien (14c.), or a native formation from music + -ian.

meanings.definitionCn: 使减到最少， 使降到最低

etymology.story: 1802, first recorded in Bentham; see minimum + -ize..

meanings.definitionCn: 下降；下去；下来；遗传；屈尊
圣人；圣徒；道德崇高的人

morphology.mnemonic: logical argumentation + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: early 15c., "based on reason," from logic + -al (1).

meanings.definitionCn: 强迫,迫使;责成;(使)感激,施恩于
摇摆；摆动；秋千；音律；涨落
摇摆；转向；悬挂；大摇大摆地行走
旋转的；悬挂的；强节奏爵士音乐的

etymology.story: 1540s, ridyculouse, from Latin ridiculus "laughable, funny, absurd," from ridere "to laugh" (see risible).
also hand-bag, "bag for small articles, carried in the hand," 1854, from hand (n.) + bag (n.)..

mid-15c., from Old French invasion "invasion, attack, assaut" (12c.), from Late Latin invasionem (nominative invasio) "an attack, invasion," noun of action from past participle stem of Latin invadere "to go, come, or get into; enter violently, penetrate into as an enemy, assail, assault, make an attack on," from in- "in" (see in- (2)) + vadere "go, walk" (see vamoose)..

meanings.definitionCn: 严峻的；严厉的；剧烈的；苛刻的

etymology.story: 1540s, from Middle French severe (12c., Modern French sévère) or directly from Latin severus "serious, grave, strict, austere" (see severity).

1560s, from Middle French émerger and directly from Latin emergere "bring forth, bring to light," intransitively "arise out or up, come forth, come up, come out, rise," from assimilated form of ex- "out" (see ex-) + mergere "to dip, sink" (see merge).

meanings.definitionCn: 租约；租期；租赁物；租赁权

etymology.story: late 14c., "legal contract conveying property, usually for a fixed period of time and with a fixed compensation," from Anglo-French les (late 13c.), Old French lais, lez "a lease, a letting, a leaving," verbal noun from Old French laissier "to let, allow, permit; bequeath, leave" (see lease (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 拍击；侮辱；掌击；掴…的耳光

etymology.story: mid-15c., probably of imitative origin, similar to Low German slappe, German Schlappe.

meanings.definitionCn: 吐痰；吐口水；发出劈啪声
搜索，侦察；侦察员；侦察机

etymology.story: early 13c., originally especially of the day of a person's death, from Medieval Latin anniversarium, from Latin anniversarius (adj.) "returning annually," from annus (genitive anni) "year" (see annual (adj.)) + versus, past participle of vertere "to turn" (see versus).

meanings.definitionCn: 登记；注册；记录；挂号邮寄；把…挂号；正式提出
登记；注册；记录；寄存器；登记簿
得不到休息的；不平静的；不安的， 坐立不安的

etymology.story: late 14c., from rest (n.1) + -less.

meanings.definitionCn: 有说服力的；劝诱的，劝说的

etymology.story: 1580s, from Middle French persuasif, from Medieval Latin persuasivus, from Latin persuas-, past participle stem of persuadere "persuade, convince" (see persuasion).

Old English fleotan "to float; drift; flow, run (as water); swim; sail (of a ship)," from Proto-Germanic *fleutan (source also of Old Frisian fliata, Old Saxon fliotan "to flow," Old High German fliozzan "to float, flow," German fliessen "to flow, run, trickle" (as water), Old Norse fliota "to float, flow"), from PIE root *pleu- "to flow,  swim" (see pluvial).

meanings.definitionCn: 传达,传送;交流;通讯,通话

etymology.story: 1520s, "to impart" (information, etc.), from Latin communicatus, past participle of communicare "impart, inform" (see communication).

meanings.definitionCn: 明显;显著;根据;证据;迹象

etymology.story: Old English sadolian "to put a riding saddle on;" see saddle (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 农村的，乡下的；田园的，有乡村风味的

etymology.story: early 15c., from Old French rural (14c.), from Latin ruralis "of the countryside," from rus (genitive ruris) "open land, country," from PIE *reue- (1) "to open; space" (see room (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: place for conducting business + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: late 14c., "performing a service; required by duty," from Old French oficial "official; main, principal" (14c., Modern French officiel) or directly from Late Latin officialis "of or belonging to duty, service, or office," from Latin officium (see office).

meanings.definitionCn: 历史的；史学的；基于史实的

root.meaning: the recorded events of the past

morphology.mnemonic: the recorded events of the past + ic + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: early 15c., "of or pertaining to history, conveying information from the past," with -al (1) + Latin historicus "of history, historical," from Greek historikos "historical; of or for inquiry," from historia (see history).

early 15c., "act of saying over again," from Old French repetition and directly from Latin repetitionem (nominative repetitio) "a repeating," noun of action from past participle stem of repetere "do or say again" (see repeat (v.)).

morphology.mnemonic: fortune + 使…/具有…的 + 以…方式/副词化

etymology.story: "by (good) fortune," 1540s, from fortunate + -ly (2)..

meanings.definitionCn: 酸,酸性物质a.酸的,酸味的；尖刻的
传动装置，[机] 变速器；传递；传送；播送

etymology.story: 1610s, "conveyance from one place to another," from Latin transmissionem (nominative transmissio) "a sending over or across, passage," noun of action from past participle stem of transmittere "send over or across" (see transmit).

morphology.mnemonic: make short + 行为/结果/集合

etymology.story: 1862, American English, from short + -age..

meanings.definitionCn: 区别， 辨别； 识别力； 差别对待， 歧视

morphology.mnemonic: 不/分离 + 判定/控告 + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1640s, "the making of distinctions," from Late Latin discriminationem (nominative discriminatio), noun of action from past participle stem of discriminare (see discriminate).

meanings.definitionCn: 女性的；雌性的；柔弱的，柔和的

etymology.story: early 14c., from Old French femelle "woman, female" (12c.), from Medieval Latin femella "a female," from Latin femella "young female, girl," diminutive of femina "woman" (see feminine).
1680s, "ear trumpet for the hard-of-hearing," coined from Greek mikros "small" (see mica) + phone "sound," from PIE root *bha- (2) "to speak, tell, say" (see fame (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 快的；迅速的；敏捷的；立刻的

etymology.story: early 15c., in law, "a giving up" (of an estate, land grant, interest in property, etc.), from Anglo-French surrendre, Old French surrendre noun use of infinitive, "give up, deliver over" (see surrender (v.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 模糊的；头晕的；虚弱的；[医] 衰弱的
昏倒；变得微弱；变得没气力
执行牧师职务；辅助或伺候某人

etymology.story: early 14c., "to perform religious rites, provide religious services;" mid-14c., "to serve (food or drink);" late 14c.

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 感觉/感知

etymology.story: 1580s, from French insister (14c.) or directly from Latin insistere "take a stand, stand on, stand still; follow, pursue; insist, press vigorously, urge, dwell upon," from in- "upon" (see in- (2)) + sistere "take a stand" (see assist (v.)).
in the mechanical sense, 1868, from type (n.) + writer.

meanings.definitionCn: 无助的； 无能的； 无法抗拒的

etymology.story: "unable to act for oneself," c.
1540s, "to regard," from Middle French respecter "look back; respect; delay," from Latin respectere, frequentative of respicere (see respect (n.).

late 14c., "roll of parchment containing writing; a bound book," from Old French volume "scroll, book; work, volume; girth, size" (13c.) and directly from Latin volumen (genitive voluminis) "roll (of a manuscript); coil, wreath," literally "that which is rolled," from volvere "to turn around, roll" (see volvox).
early 15c., æche, from Old English æce, from Proto-Germanic *akiz, from same source as ache (v.)..
short for mutual fund, 1971; see mutual..

meanings.definitionCn: 剥落；设定命令行解释器的位置

etymology.story: Old English sciell, scill, Anglian scell "seashell, eggshell," related to Old English scealu "shell, husk," from Proto-Germanic *skaljo "piece cut off; shell; scale" (source also of West Frisian skyl "peel, rind," Middle Low German schelle "pod, rind, egg shell," Gothic skalja "tile"), with the shared notion of "covering that splits off," from PIE root *(s)kel- (1) "to cut, cleave" (source also of Old Church Slavonic skolika "shell," Russian skala "bark, rind;" see scale (n.1)).
1785, "line marked on a surface before cutting," from guide + line (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 宽松的；散漫的；不牢固的；不精确的

etymology.story: Old English þærfore; from there + fore, Old English and Middle English collateral form of for.

morphology.mnemonic: 出/向外/前任 + a different situation

etymology.story: late 15c., from Old French eschangier "exchange, barter" (Modern French échanger), from Vulgar Latin *excambiare (source of Italian scambiare); see exchange (n.).
1620s, from Latin haesitatus, past participle of haesitare "to stick fast; to hesitate; to stammer" (see hesitation).

1775, "portable firearm having a spirally grooved bore," used earlier of the grooves themselves (1751), noun use of rifled (pistol), 1680s, from verb meaning "to cut spiral grooves in" (a gun barrel); see rifle (v.2)..

meanings.definitionCn: 私人的,个人的,秘密的,私下的

etymology.story: 1590s, "private citizen," short for private person "individual not involved in government" (early 15c.), or from Latin privatus "man in private life," noun use of the adjective; 1781 in the military sense, short for Private soldier "one below the rank of a non-commissioned officer" (1570s), from private (adj.)..

meanings.definitionCn: 行李；[交] 辎重（军队的）

etymology.story: mid-15c., "portable equipment of an army; plunder, loot," from Old French bagage "baggage, (military) equipment" (14c.), from bague "pack, bundle, sack," ultimately from the same Scandinavian source that yielded bag (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 故障；崩溃；分解；分类；衰弱；跺脚曳步舞

etymology.story: "a collapse," 1832, from break (v.) + down (adv.).

meanings.definitionCn: 面团，膏；糊状物，[胶粘] 浆糊

etymology.story: "to come up to, to catch in pursuit," early 13c., from over- + take (v.).

root.meaning: gang of workers employed after dark

morphology.mnemonic: 过度/超越 + gang of workers employed after dark

etymology.story: early 14c., from over- + night (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 表现；（机器等）运转；举止端正；（事物）起某种作用

etymology.story: early 15c., from be- intensive prefix + have in sense of "to have or bear (oneself) in a particular way, comport" (compare German sich behaben, French se porter).

meanings.definitionCn: 伸展,张开；（大量地）使用，消耗（金钱，时间）；使竭尽所能；使全力以赴；

etymology.story: Old English streccan (transitive and intransitive) "to stretch, spread out, prostrate; reach, extend" (past tense strehte, past participle streht), from Proto-Germanic *strakjanan (source also of Danish strække, Swedish sträcka, Old Frisian strekka, Old High German strecchan, Middle Low German, Middle Dutch, Old High German, German strecken "to stretch, draw out"), perhaps a variant of the root of stark, or else from PIE root *strenk- "tight, narrow; pull tight, twist" (see string (n.)).

Old English undon "to unfasten and open" (a window or door), "to unfasten by releasing from a fixed position; to cancel, discharge, abrogate, reverse what has been done, put back in a former condition; bring to ruin, destroy," from un- (2) "opposite of" + do (v.).

late 14c., from Old French acompliss-, present participle stem of acomplir "to fulfill, fill up, complete" (12c.), from Vulgar Latin *accomplere, from Latin ad- "to" (see ad-) + complere "fill up" (see complete (adj.)).
1960, acronym for "light amplification by stimulated emission of radiation," on pattern of maser (1955).
early 15c., from Old French misture and directly from Latin mixtura "a mixing," from mixtus (see mix (v.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 引擎，发动机；机车，火车头；工具
应负责的， 有责任的；可靠的， 可信赖的；责任重大的， 重要的

etymology.story: 1590s, "answerable" (to another, for something), from obsolete French responsible (13c., Modern French responsable, as if from Latin *responsabilis), from Latin respons-, past participle stem of respondere "to respond" (see respond).

meanings.definitionCn: 范围；余地；视野；眼界；导弹射程

etymology.story: "to view," 1807, from the source of scope (n.2).

1540s, "follower or attendant of a superior person," from Middle French satellite (14c.), from Latin satellitem (nominative satelles) "attendant, companion, courtier, accomplice, assistant," perhaps from Etruscan satnal (Klein), or a compound of roots *satro- "full, enough" + *leit- (1) "to go" (Tucker); compare English follow, which is constructed of similar roots.

meanings.definitionCn: 子弹；只选某党全部候选人的投票；豆子

etymology.story: 1550s, from Middle French boulette "cannonball, small ball," diminutive of boule "a ball" (13c.), from Latin bulla "round thing, knob" (see bull (n.2)).

meanings.definitionCn: 录音机；记录器；记录员；八孔直笛

morphology.mnemonic: written account of some event + er

etymology.story: "chief legal officer of a city," early 15c., from Anglo-French recordour (early 14c.), Old French recordeor "witness; storyteller; minstrel," from Medieval Latin recordator, from Latin recordari "remember" (see record (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 识别,鉴别;(with)把…和…看成一样

morphology.mnemonic: 相同/识别 + 使…化/造成

etymology.story: 1640s, "regard as the same," from French identifier, from identité (see identity).
"to affix a label to," c.

meanings.definitionCn: 改进,进步,增进;改进措施

root.meaning: make better, raise to a better quality or condition

morphology.mnemonic: make better, raise to a better quality or condition + 结果/过程/状态

etymology.story: mid-15c., enprowment "profitable use, management of something for profit," from Anglo-French emprowement, from emprouwer "turn to profit" (see improve).

morphology.mnemonic: insert surreptitiously + er + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: "having a slippery surface," c.

meanings.definitionCn: 剪开；删除（scissor的第三人称单数）

etymology.story: late 14c., sisoures, from Old French cisoires (plural) "shears," from Vulgar Latin *cisoria (plural) "cutting instrument," from *cisus (in compounds such as Latin excisus, past participle of excidere "to cut out"), ultimately from Latin caedere "to cut" (see -cide).

meanings.definitionCn: 意义,含义;重要性,重要的

morphology.mnemonic: 标记 + 使…化/造成 + ance

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French modifier (14c.), from Latin modificare "to limit, measure off, restrain," from modus "measure, manner" (see mode (n.1)) + root of facere "to make" (see factitious).

meanings.definitionCn: [天] 地平线；视野；眼界；范围

etymology.story: late 14c., orisoun, from Old French orizon (14c., Modern French horizon), earlier orizonte (13c.), from Latin horizontem (nominative horizon), from Greek horizon (kyklos) "bounding (circle)," from horizein "bound, limit, divide, separate," from horos "boundary, landmark, marking stones." The h- was restored in English 17c.

1907, from air (n.1) + plane (n.1); though the original references are British, the word caught on in American English, where it largely superseded earlier aeroplane (1873 in this sense and still common in British English).

meanings.definitionCn: 保证，誓言；抵押；抵押品，典当物
保证，许诺；用……抵押；举杯祝……健康

morphology.mnemonic: 向前/支持 + 步/走 + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: 1680s, "a place where something tends to collect," originally figurative, from French réservoir "storehouse," from Old French reserver "to reserve" (see reserve (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 故意的；深思熟虑的；从容的

etymology.story: 1540s, from Latin deliberatus, past participle of deliberare (see deliberation).

Old English swerian "take an oath" (class VI strong verb; past tense swor, past participle sworen), from Proto-Germanic *swarjan-, (source also of Old Saxon swerian, Old Frisian swera, Old Norse sverja, Danish sverge, Middle Dutch swaren, Old High German swerien, German schwören, Gothic swaren "to swear"), from PIE root *swer- (1) "to speak, talk, say" (source also of Old Church Slavonic svara "quarrel," Oscan sverrunei "to the speaker").
1630s, atmosphaera (modern form from 1670s), from Modern Latin atmosphaera, from atmo-, comb.

early 15c., from Latin decoratus, past participle of decorare "to decorate, adorn, embellish, beautify," from decus (genitive decoris) "an ornament," from PIE root *dek- "to receive, be suitable" (see decent).
"the right to make or sell copies," 1729, from copy (v.) + right (n.).

morphology.mnemonic: 生命/生物 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: also bio-technology, 1947, "use of machinery in relation to human needs;" 1972 in sense of "use of biological processes in industrial production," from bio- + technology..

meanings.definitionCn: (个人的)事业；专业，生涯，职业，经历
占领,占据;占用;职业,工作

etymology.story: early 14c., "fact of holding or possessing;" mid-14c., "a being employed in something," also "a particular action," from Old French occupacion "pursuit, work, employment; occupancy, occupation" (12c.), from Latin occupationem (nominative occupatio) "a taking possession; business, employment," noun of action from past participle stem of occupare (see occupy).
1620s, from Medieval Latin tendentia "inclination, leaning," from Latin tendens, present participle of tendere "to stretch, extend, aim" (see tenet).

meanings.definitionCn: 释放；发射；让与；允许发表

etymology.story: early 14c., "abatement of distress; means of deliverance," from Old French relais, reles (12c.), a back-formation from relesser, relaissier (see release (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 特许；包租；发给特许执照
苗条的；修长的；微小的；差的
(Slim)人名；(阿拉伯)萨利姆；(英、西)斯利姆

etymology.story: mid-13c., arsmetike, from Old French arsmetique (12c.), from Latin arithmetica, from Greek arithmetike (tekhne) "(the) counting (art)," fem.
"containing faults, errors, or defects," late 14c., from fault (n.) + -y (2).
1620s, from Latin liberatus, past participle of liberare "set free," from liber "free" (see liberal).

meanings.definitionCn: 变化；[生物] 变异，变种

etymology.story: late 14c., "difference, divergence," from Old French variacion "variety, diversity" and directly from Latin variationem (nominative variatio) "a difference, variation, change," from past participle stem of variare "to change" (see vary).

meanings.definitionCn: 道德的；精神上的；品性端正的

morphology.mnemonic: 向下/移除/取消 + 做/制造

etymology.story: 1570s, from Latin defectus, past participle of deficere "to fail, desert" (see defect (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + in + bring about by unusual effort
出/向外/前任 + 爆发/鼓掌

1856, of mental positions; 1858 in a physical sense, from view + point (n.)..

Old English forð "forward, onward, further; continually;" as a preposition, "during," perfective of fore, from Proto-Germanic *furtha- "forward" (source also of Old Frisian, Old Saxon forth "forward, onward," Old Norse forð, Dutch voort, German fort), from extended form of PIE root *per- (1) "forward, through" (see per).

Old English wiðstandan "resist, oppose," from wið "against" (see with) + standan "to stand" (see stand (v.)); perhaps a loan-translation of Latin resistere "to resist" (see resist).

etymology.story: 1716, earlier aera (1610s), from Late Latin aera, era "an era or epoch from which time is reckoned" (7c.), probably identical with Latin aera "counters used for calculation," plural of aes (genitive aeris) "brass, copper, money" (see ore, also compare copper).
Old English gield "payment, sum of money; service, offering, worship;" from the source of yield (v.).
1901, "pertaining to electrons;" see electron + -ic; 1930 as "pertaining to electronics."

late 15c., "tastefully ornate," from Middle French élégant (15c.), from Latin elegantem (nominative elegans) "choice, fine, tasteful," collateral form of present participle of eligere "select with care, choose." Meaning "characterized by refined grace" is from 1520s.

etymology.story: 1824, from Modern Latin transferred use of classical Latin curriculum "a running, course, career" (also "a fast chariot, racing car"), from currere (see current (adj.)).
also mean time, mid-14c., from mean (adj.2) "middle, intermediate" + time (n.).

etymology.story: early 14c., "to escape, to move softly and quickly," from an unrecorded Old English word or cognate Middle Low German slippen "to glide, slide," from Proto-Germanic *slipan (source also of Old High German slifan, Middle Dutch slippen, German schleifen "to glide, slide"), from PIE *sleib-, from root *(s)lei- "slimy, sticky, slippery" (see slime (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 锚；抛锚停泊；靠山；新闻节目主播

etymology.story: mid-15c., "to summon," from Old French citer "to summon" (14c.), from Latin citare "to summon, urge, call; put in sudden motion, call forward; rouse, excite," frequentative of ciere "to move, set in motion, stir, rouse, call, invite" from PIE root *keie- "to set in motion, to move to and fro" (source also of Sanskrit cyavate "stirs himself, goes;" Greek kinein "to move, set in motion; change, stir up," kinymai "move myself;" Gothic haitan "call, be called;" Old English hatan "command, call").
mid-14c., "branch of philosophy that treats of forms of thinking," from Old French logique (13c.), from Latin (ars) logica, from Greek logike (techne) "reasoning (art)," from fem.

etymology.story: 1899, from intern (n.) + -ship..

meanings.definitionCn: 流出；发散， 散发；脱落； 摆脱

etymology.story:
"cast off," Old English sceadan, scadan "to divide, separate, part company; discriminate, decide; scatter abroad, cast about," strong verb (past tense scead, past participle sceadan), from Proto-Germanic *skaithan (source also of Old Saxon skethan, Old Frisian sketha, Middle Dutch sceiden, Dutch scheiden, Old High German sceidan, German scheiden "part, separate, distinguish," Gothic skaidan "separate"), from *skaith "divide, split."

According to Klein's sources, this probably is related to PIE root *skei- "to cut, separate, divide, part, split" (source also of Sanskrit chid-, Greek skhizein, Latin scindere "to split;" Lithuanian skedzu "I make thin, separate, divide;" Old Irish scian "knife;" Welsh chwydu "to break open").

1630s, "a cutting off (mentally), abstraction," from French précision (16c.) and directly from Latin praecisionem (nominative praecisio) "a cutting off," noun of action from past participle stem of praecidere (see precise).
"to shield from punishment, to conceal," late 15c., from screen (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 裸体的；无装饰的；无证据的；直率的

etymology.story: Old English nacod "nude, bare; empty," also "not fully clothed," from Proto-Germanic *nakwadaz (source also of Old Frisian nakad, Middle Dutch naket, Dutch naakt, Old High German nackot, German nackt, Old Norse nökkviðr, Old Swedish nakuþer, Gothic naqaþs "naked"), from PIE root *nogw- "naked" (source also of Sanskrit nagna, Hittite nekumant-, Old Persian *nagna-, Greek gymnos, Latin nudus, Lithuanian nuogas, Old Church Slavonic nagu-, Russian nagoi, Old Irish nocht, Welsh noeth "bare, naked").
mid-15c., "act or process of testing, a putting to proof by examination, experiment, etc.," from Anglo-French trial, noun formed from triet "to try" (see try (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 通信；符合， 一致；相当于， 对应

etymology.story: 1520s, "to be in agreement, to be in harmony with," from Middle French correspondre (14c.) or directly from Medieval Latin correspondere, from cor- (see com-) "together, with each other" + respondere "to answer" (see respond).

meanings.definitionCn: 看法； 感觉； 洞察力

morphology.mnemonic: 通过/彻底 + 取/抓 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., "receiving, collection," from Latin perceptionem (nominative perceptio) "perception, apprehension, a taking," from percipere "perceive" (see perceive).

meanings.definitionCn: 承担，保证；从事；同意；试图

etymology.story: 1690s, "desk with drawers, writing desk," from French bureau "office; desk, writing table," originally "cloth covering for a desk," from burel "coarse woolen cloth" (as a cover for writing desks), Old French diminutive of bure "dark brown cloth," which is perhaps either from Latin burrus "red," or from Late Latin burra "wool, shaggy garment." Offices being full of such desks, the meaning expanded 1720 to "division of a government." Meaning "chest of drawers" is from 1770, said to be American English but early in British use..

late 14c., "moist, wet; well-irrigated," from Old French moiste "damp, wet, soaked" (13c., Modern French moite), from Vulgar Latin *muscidus "moldy," also "wet," from Latin mucidus "slimy, moldy, musty," from mucus "slime" (see mucus).

meanings.definitionCn: 限制；使具有资格；证明…合格

meanings.definitionCn: 强烈的；紧张的；非常的；热情的

etymology.story: early 15c., of situations or qualities, "great, extreme," from Old French intense (13c.), from Latin intensus "stretched, strained, high-strung, tight," originally past participle of intendere in its literal sense of "stretch out, strain" (see intend).

etymology.story: "one who lives at the same time as another," 1630s, originally cotemporary, from co- + temporary; modified by influence of contemporary (adj.).

适合；搭上齿轮；开始工作

1620s, from bound (n.) + -ary..

late 12c., from Old French rober "rob, steal, pillage, ransack, rape," from West Germanic *rauba "booty" (source also of Old High German roubon "to rob," roub "spoil, plunder;" Old English reafian, source of the reave in bereave), from Proto-Germanic *raubon "to rob," from PIE *reup-, *reub- "to snatch" (see rip (v.)).To rob the cradle is attested from 1864 in reference to drafting young men in the American Civil War; by 1949 in reference to seductions or romantic relationships with younger persons.

meanings.definitionCn: 上了年纪的；过了中年的；稍老的

Old English rodd "a rod, pole," which is probably cognate with Old Norse rudda "club," from Proto-Germanic *rudd- "stick, club," from PIE *reudh- "to clear land."

As a long, tapering elastic pole for fishing, from mid-15c.

meanings.definitionCn: 哑的，无说话能力的；不说话的，无声音的

etymology.story:
Old English dumb "silent, unable to speak," from PIE *dheubh- "confusion, stupefaction, dizziness," from root *dheu- (1) "dust, mist, vapor, smoke," and related notions of "defective perception or wits."

The Old English, Old Saxon (dumb), Gothic (dumbs), and Old Norse (dumbr) forms of the word meant only "mute, speechless;" in Old High German (thumb) it meant both this and "stupid," and in Modern German this latter became the only sense.

etymology.story: late 14c., "to specify; to end," from Old French defenir, definir "to finish, conclude, come to an end; bring to an end; define, determine with precision," and directly from Latin definire "to limit, determine, explain," from de- "completely" (see de-) + finire "to bound, limit," from finis "boundary, end" (see finish (v.)).
1590s, from Latin dictatum "something dictated," noun use of neuter past participle of dictare (see dictate (v.))..

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 建造 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French construction or directly from Latin constructionem (nominative constructio), from construct-, past participle stem of construere "pile up together, accumulate; build, make, erect," from com- "together" (see com-) + struere "to pile up" (see structure (n.))..

1620s (implied in predicted), "foretell, prophesy," a back formation from prediction or else from Latin praedicatus, past participle of praedicere "foretell, advise, give notice," from prae "before" (see pre-) + dicere "to say" (see diction).

致使；提出；实施；着色；以…回报

mid-13c., "widely apart," from Old English on brede, which meant something like "at wide" (see a- (1) + broad (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 解除，减轻；使不单调乏味；换…的班；解围；使放心

etymology.story: late 14c., "alleviate (pain, etc.), mitigate; afford comfort; allow respite; diminish the pressure of," also "give alms to, provide for;" also figuratively, "take heart, cheer up;" from Old French relever "to raise, relieve" (11c.) and directly from Latin relevare "to raise, alleviate, lift up, free from a burden," from re-, intensive prefix (see re-), + levare "to lift up, lighten," from levis "not heavy" (see lever).

meanings.definitionCn: 抽屉；开票人；出票人；起草者；酒馆侍

meanings.definitionCn: 运输,运送,搬运n.运输;运输系统,运载工具

mid-15c., "the spending of money," from Middle English emploien (see employ) + -ment..

meanings.definitionCn: 明确的,具体的;特定的,特有的
应用；申请；应用程序；敷用

morphology.mnemonic: 统治 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: early 15c., "the bringing of something to bear on something else," from Old French aplicacion (14c.), from Latin applicationem (nominative applicatio) "a joining to, an attaching oneself to," noun of action from past participle stem of applicare (see apply).

meanings.definitionCn: 吸引，占用；使参加；雇佣；使订婚；预定

etymology.story: early 15c., "to pledge" (something, as security for payment), from Old French engagier "bind (by promise or oath), pledge; pawn" (12c.), from phrase en gage "under pledge," from en "in" (see en- (1)) + gage "pledge," through Frankish from Proto-Germanic *wadiare "pledge" (see wed).

Old English æcer "tilled field, open land," from Proto-Germanic *akraz "field, pasture" (source also of Old Norse akr, Old Saxon akkar, Old Frisian ekker, Middle Dutch acker, Dutch akker, Old High German achar, German acker, Gothic akrs), from PIE *agro- "field" (source also of Latin ager "field, land," Greek agros, Sanskrit ajras "plain, open country").

meanings.definitionCn: 食欲，胃口；欲望，性欲；爱好，趣味

etymology.story: 1827, from German Semester "half-year course in a university," from Latin semestris, in cursus semestris "course of six months," from semestris, semenstris "of six months, lasting six months, half-yearly, semi-annual," from sex "six" (see six) + mensis "month" (see moon (n.)).

1810, "art of a general," from French stratégie (18c.) and directly from Greek strategia "office or command of a general," from strategos "general, commander of an army," also the title of various civil officials and magistrates, from stratos "multitude, army, expedition, encamped army," literally "that which is spread out" (see structure (n.)) + agos "leader," from agein "to lead" (see act (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 钝的；迟钝的；无趣的；呆滞的；阴暗的

etymology.story: 1590s, "acting as if benumbed," alteration of Middle English clumsid "numb with cold" (14c.), past participle of clumsen "to benumb, stiffen or paralyze with cold or fear," from a Scandinavian source akin to Old Norse klumsa "make speechless, palsy; prevent from speaking," intensive of kluma "to make motionless." For insertion of -s-, see flimsy.

meanings.definitionCn: 领土，领域；范围；地域；版图
